title: Alfred Hitchcock

infobox.value: Alfred Joseph Hitchcock  (1899-08-13)13 August 1899  [Leytonstone](/wiki/Leytonstone "Leytonstone"), Essex, England
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of awards and nominations received by Alfred Hitchcock")

(13 August 1899 – 29 April 1980) was an English filmmaker. He is widely regarded as one of the greatest and most influential figures in the [history of cinema](/wiki/History_of_cinema "History of cinema").[[1]](#cite_note-influential-1) In a career spanning six decades, he directed [over 50 feature films](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_filmography "Alfred Hitchcock filmography"),[[a]](#cite_note-4) many of which are still widely watched and studied today. Known as the "**Master of Suspense**", Hitchcock became as well known as any of his actors thanks to his many interviews, [his cameo appearances](/wiki/List_of_cameo_appearances_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of cameo appearances by Alfred Hitchcock") in most of his films, and his hosting and producing the television anthology *[Alfred Hitchcock Presents](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents "Alfred Hitchcock Presents")* (1955–65). Among [other accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of awards and nominations received by Alfred Hitchcock"), his films garnered 46 [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nominations, including six wins, although he never won the award for [Best Director](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Director "Academy Award for Best Director"), despite five nominations.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

English filmmaker (1899–1980)

"Hitchcock" and "Master of Suspense" redirect here. For the album, see [*Master of Suspense* (album)](/wiki/Master_of_Suspense_(album) "Master of Suspense (album)"). For the police officer, see [Alf Hitchcock](/wiki/Alf_Hitchcock "Alf Hitchcock"). For other uses, see [Hitchcock (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hitchcock_(disambiguation) "Hitchcock (disambiguation)").

**Sir Alfred Joseph Hitchcock** (13 August 1899 – 29 April 1980) was an English filmmaker. He is widely regarded as one of the greatest and most influential figures in the [history of cinema](/wiki/History_of_cinema "History of cinema").[[1]](#cite_note-influential-1) In a career spanning six decades, he directed [over 50 feature films](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_filmography "Alfred Hitchcock filmography"),[[a]](#cite_note-4) many of which are still widely watched and studied today. Known as the "**Master of Suspense**", Hitchcock became as well known as any of his actors thanks to his many interviews, [his cameo appearances](/wiki/List_of_cameo_appearances_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of cameo appearances by Alfred Hitchcock") in most of his films, and his hosting and producing the television anthology *[Alfred Hitchcock Presents](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents "Alfred Hitchcock Presents")* (1955–65). Among [other accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of awards and nominations received by Alfred Hitchcock"), his films garnered 46 [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") nominations, including six wins, although he never won the award for [Best Director](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Director "Academy Award for Best Director"), despite five nominations.

Hitchcock initially trained as a technical clerk and copywriter before entering the film industry in 1919 as a [title card](/wiki/Title_card "Title card") designer. His directorial debut was the British–German [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") *[The Pleasure Garden](/wiki/The_Pleasure_Garden_(1925_film) "The Pleasure Garden (1925 film)")* (1926). His first successful film, *[The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog](/wiki/The_Lodger:_A_Story_of_the_London_Fog "The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog")* (1927), helped to shape the [thriller](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") genre, and *[Blackmail](/wiki/Blackmail_(1929_film) "Blackmail (1929 film)")* (1929) was the first British "[talkie](/wiki/Sound_film#Transition:_Europe "Sound film")".[[4]](#cite_note-Blackmail-5) His thrillers *[The 39 Steps](/wiki/The_39_Steps_(1935_film) "The 39 Steps (1935 film)")* (1935) and *[The Lady Vanishes](/wiki/The_Lady_Vanishes "The Lady Vanishes")* (1938) are ranked among the [greatest British films of the 20th century](/wiki/BFI_Top_100_British_films "BFI Top 100 British films"). By 1939, he had earned international recognition, and producer [David O. Selznick](/wiki/David_O._Selznick "David O. Selznick") persuaded him to move to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"). A string of successful films followed, including *[Rebecca](/wiki/Rebecca_(1940_film) "Rebecca (1940 film)")* (1940), *[Foreign Correspondent](/wiki/Foreign_Correspondent_(film) "Foreign Correspondent (film)")* (1940), *[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")* (1941), *[Shadow of a Doubt](/wiki/Shadow_of_a_Doubt "Shadow of a Doubt")* (1943) and *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(1946_film) "Notorious (1946 film)")* (1946). *Rebecca* won the [Academy Award for Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture#1940s "Academy Award for Best Picture"), with Hitchcock nominated as Best Director.[[5]](#cite_note-Rebecca-6) He also received Oscar nominations for *[Lifeboat](/wiki/Lifeboat_(1944_film) "Lifeboat (1944 film)")* (1944), *[Spellbound](/wiki/Spellbound_(1945_film) "Spellbound (1945 film)")* (1945), *[Rear Window](/wiki/Rear_Window "Rear Window")* (1954) and *[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)")* (1960).[[6]](#cite_note-AFI_top_100-7)

Hitchcock's other notable films include *[Rope](/wiki/Rope_(film) "Rope (film)")* (1948), *[Strangers on a Train](/wiki/Strangers_on_a_Train_(film) "Strangers on a Train (film)")* (1951), *[Dial M for Murder](/wiki/Dial_M_for_Murder "Dial M for Murder")* (1954), *[To Catch a Thief](/wiki/To_Catch_a_Thief "To Catch a Thief")* (1955), *[The Trouble with Harry](/wiki/The_Trouble_with_Harry "The Trouble with Harry")* (1955), *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* (1958), *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")* (1959), *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(film) "The Birds (film)")* (1963), [*Marnie*](/wiki/Marnie_(film) "Marnie (film)") (1964) and *[Frenzy](/wiki/Frenzy "Frenzy")* (1972), all of which were also financially successful and are highly regarded by film historians. Hitchcock made a number of films with some of the biggest stars in Hollywood, including four with [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant"), four with [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart"), three with [Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman") and three consecutively with [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly"). Hitchcock became an American citizen in 1955.

In 2012, Hitchcock's psychological thriller *Vertigo*, starring Stewart, displaced [Orson Welles](/wiki/Orson_Welles "Orson Welles")' *[Citizen Kane](/wiki/Citizen_Kane "Citizen Kane")* (1941) as the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute")'s greatest film ever made based on its world-wide poll of hundreds of film critics.[[7]](#cite_note-Christie2012-8) As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit), nine of his films had been selected for preservation in the United States [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry"),[[b]](#cite_note-10) including his personal favourite, *Shadow of a Doubt* (1943).[[c]](#cite_note-12) He received the [BAFTA Fellowship](/wiki/BAFTA_Fellowship "BAFTA Fellowship") in 1971, the [AFI Life Achievement Award](/wiki/AFI_Life_Achievement_Award "AFI Life Achievement Award") in 1979, and was [knighted](/wiki/Knight_Bachelor "Knight Bachelor") in December of that year, four months before his death on 29 April 1980.[[10]](#cite_note-Todd30April1980-13)

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

### Early life: 1899–1919

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life: 1899–1919")]

#### Early childhood and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early childhood and education")]

Alfred Joseph Hitchcock was born on 13 August 1899 at the flat above his parents' leased [greengrocer](/wiki/Greengrocer "Greengrocer")'s shop at 517 High Road in [Leytonstone](/wiki/Leytonstone "Leytonstone"), which was then part of [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex") (now part of the [London Borough of Waltham Forest](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Waltham_Forest "London Borough of Waltham Forest")). He was the son of greengrocer and poulterer, William Edgar Hitchcock (1862–1914) and Emma Jane (née Whelan; 1863–1942). The household was "characterised by an atmosphere of discipline".[[11]](#cite_note-14) He had an older brother named William John (1888–1943) and an older sister named Ellen Kathleen (1892–1979) who used the nickname "Nellie". His parents were both [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") with English and Irish ancestry.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair200211–12-15)[[13]](#cite_note-16) His father was a greengrocer, as his grandfather had been.[[14]](#cite_note-17) There was a large extended family, including uncle John Hitchcock with his five-bedroom Victorian house on Campion Road in [Putney](/wiki/Putney "Putney"), complete with a maid, cook, chauffeur, and gardener. Every summer, his uncle rented a seaside house for the family in [Cliftonville](/wiki/Cliftonville "Cliftonville"), Kent. Hitchcock said that he first became class-conscious there, noticing the differences between tourists and locals.[[15]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Site_of_517_High_Road_Leytonstone_London_E11_3EE_%28Birthplace_of_Alfred_Hitchcock%29.jpg/250px-Site_of_517_High_Road_Leytonstone_London_E11_3EE_%28Birthplace_of_Alfred_Hitchcock%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Site_of_517_High_Road_Leytonstone_London_E11_3EE_(Birthplace_of_Alfred_Hitchcock).jpg)

Petrol station at the site of 517 High Road, [Leytonstone](/wiki/Leytonstone "Leytonstone"), where Hitchcock was born; commemorative mural at nos. 527–533 *(right)*[[16]](#cite_note-19)

Describing himself as a well-behaved boy – his father called him his "little lamb without a spot" – Hitchcock said he could not remember ever having had a playmate.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198325-20) One of his favourite stories for interviewers was about his father sending him to the local police station with a note when he was five; the policeman looked at the note and locked him in a cell for a few minutes, saying, "This is what we do to naughty boys." The experience left him with a lifelong phobia of law enforcement, and he told [Tom Snyder](/wiki/Tom_Snyder "Tom Snyder") in 1973 that he was "scared stiff of anything ... to do with the law" and that he would refuse to even drive a car in case he got a parking ticket.[[18]](#cite_note-21) When he was six, the family moved to [Limehouse](/wiki/Limehouse "Limehouse") and leased two stores at 130 and 175 Salmon Lane, which they ran as a [fish-and-chip](/wiki/Fish-and-chip "Fish-and-chip") shop and fishmongers' respectively; they lived above the former.[[19]](#cite_note-McG2003p13-22) Hitchcock attended his first school, the Howrah House Convent in [Poplar](/wiki/Poplar,_London "Poplar, London"), which he entered in 1907, at age 7.[[20]](#cite_note-23) According to biographer [Patrick McGilligan](/wiki/Patrick_McGilligan_(biographer) "Patrick McGilligan (biographer)"), he stayed at Howrah House for at most two years. He also attended a convent school, the Wode Street School "for the daughters of gentlemen and little boys" run by the [Faithful Companions of Jesus](/wiki/Faithful_Companions_of_Jesus "Faithful Companions of Jesus"). He then attended a primary school near his home and was for a short time a boarder at [Salesian College](/wiki/Salesian_College,_Battersea "Salesian College, Battersea") in [Battersea](/wiki/Battersea "Battersea").[[21]](#cite_note-24)

The family moved again when Hitchcock was eleven, this time to [Stepney](/wiki/Stepney "Stepney"), and on 5 October 1910 he was sent to [St Ignatius College](/wiki/St_Ignatius%27_College "St Ignatius' College") in [Stamford Hill](/wiki/Stamford_Hill "Stamford Hill"), a [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") grammar school with a reputation for discipline.[[22]](#cite_note-25) As corporal punishment, the priests used a flat, hard, springy tool made of [gutta-percha](/wiki/Gutta-percha "Gutta-percha") and known as a "ferula" which struck the whole palm; punishment was always at the end of the day, so the boys had to sit through classes anticipating the punishment if they had been written up for it. He later said that this is where he developed his sense of fear.[[23]](#cite_note-26) The school register lists his year of birth as 1900 rather than 1899; biographer [Donald Spoto](/wiki/Donald_Spoto "Donald Spoto") says he was deliberately enrolled as a ten-year-old because he was a year behind with his schooling.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto199923–24-27) While biographer Gene Adair reports that Hitchcock was "an average, or slightly above-average, pupil",[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair200215-28) Hitchcock said that he was "usually among the four or five at the top of the class";[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198326-29) at the end of his first year, his work in Latin, English, French and [religious education](/wiki/Religious_education "Religious education") was noted.[[27]](#cite_note-30) He told [Peter Bogdanovich](/wiki/Peter_Bogdanovich "Peter Bogdanovich"): "The Jesuits taught me organisation, control and, to some degree, analysis."[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair200215-28)

Hitchcock's favourite subject was geography and he became interested in maps and the timetables of trains, trams and buses; according to [John Russell Taylor](/wiki/John_Russell_Taylor "John Russell Taylor"), he could recite all the stops on the *[Orient Express](/wiki/Orient_Express "Orient Express")*.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor199631-31) He had a particular interest in [London trams](/wiki/Trams_in_London "Trams in London"). An overwhelming majority of his films include rail or tram scenes, in particular *[The Lady Vanishes](/wiki/The_Lady_Vanishes "The Lady Vanishes")*, *[Strangers on a Train](/wiki/Strangers_on_a_Train_(film) "Strangers on a Train (film)")* and *[Number Seventeen](/wiki/Number_Seventeen "Number Seventeen")*. A [clapperboard](/wiki/Clapperboard "Clapperboard") shows the number of the scene and the number of takes, and Hitchcock would often take the two numbers on the clapperboard and whisper the London tram route names. For example, if the clapperboard showed "Scene 23; Take 3", he would whisper "[Woodford](/wiki/Woodford,_London "Woodford, London"), [Hampstead](/wiki/Hampstead "Hampstead")"—Woodford being the terminus of the route 23 tram, and Hampstead the end of route 3.[[29]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-33)

#### Henley's

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Henley's")]

Hitchcock told his parents that he wanted to be an engineer,[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198326-29) and on 25 July 1913,[[31]](#cite_note-Spoto1999p23-34) he left St Ignatius and enrolled in night classes at the London County Council School of Engineering and Navigation in Poplar. In a [book-length interview](/wiki/Hitchcock/Truffaut "Hitchcock/Truffaut") in 1962, he told [François Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut") that he had studied "mechanics, electricity, acoustics, and navigation".[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198326-29) Then, on 12 December 1914, his father, who had been suffering from [emphysema](/wiki/Chronic_obstructive_pulmonary_disease "Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease") and kidney disease, died at the age of 52.[[32]](#cite_note-35) To support himself and his mother – his older siblings had left home by then – Hitchcock took a job, for 15 [shillings](/wiki/Shilling_(British_coin) "Shilling (British coin)") a week (£72 in 2023),[[33]](#cite_note-inflation-UK-36) as a technical clerk at the [Henley Telegraph and Cable Company](/wiki/William_Thomas_Henley "William Thomas Henley") in Blomfield Street, near [London Wall](/wiki/London_Wall "London Wall").[[34]](#cite_note-37) He continued night classes, this time in art history, painting, economics and political science.[[35]](#cite_note-38) His older brother ran the family shops, while he and his mother continued to live in Salmon Lane.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAckroyd201511-39)

Hitchcock was too young to enlist when the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") started in July 1914, and when he reached the required age of 18 in 1917, he received a C3 classification ("free from serious organic disease, able to stand service conditions in garrisons at home ... only suitable for sedentary work").[[37]](#cite_note-40) He joined a cadet regiment of the [Royal Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Engineers "Royal Engineers") and took part in theoretical briefings, weekend drills and exercises. John Russell Taylor wrote that, in one session of practical exercises in [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London"), Hitchcock was required to wear [puttees](/wiki/Puttees "Puttees"). He could never master wrapping them around his legs, and they repeatedly fell down around his ankles.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor199628-41)

After the war, Hitchcock took an interest in creative writing. In June 1919, he became a founding editor and business manager of Henley's in-house publication, *The Henley Telegraph* (sixpence a copy), to which he submitted several short stories.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200330-42)[[d]](#cite_note-50) Henley's promoted him to the advertising department, where he wrote copy and drew graphics for electric cable advertisements. He enjoyed the job and would stay late at the office to examine the proofs; he told Truffaut that this was his "first step toward cinema".[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198326-29)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor199621-51) He enjoyed watching films, especially American cinema, and from the age of 16 read the trade papers; he watched [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), [D. W. Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith") and [Buster Keaton](/wiki/Buster_Keaton "Buster Keaton"), and particularly liked [Fritz Lang](/wiki/Fritz_Lang "Fritz Lang")'s *[Der müde Tod](/wiki/Der_m%C3%BCde_Tod "Der müde Tod")* (released in Britain in 1921 as *Destiny*).[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198326-29)

### Inter-war career: 1919–1939

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Inter-war career: 1919–1939")]

#### Famous Players–Lasky

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Famous Players–Lasky")]

[![An early 1920s image of Hitchcock while directing his film titled Number 13](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Number_13.jpg/250px-Number_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Number_13.jpg)

Hitchcock (right) during the making of *[Number 13](/wiki/Number_13_(1922_film) "Number 13 (1922 film)")* in London

While still at Henley's, he read in a trade paper that [Famous Players–Lasky](/wiki/Famous_Players%E2%80%93Lasky "Famous Players–Lasky"), the production arm of [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), was opening a studio in London.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198327-52) They were planning to film *[The Sorrows of Satan](/wiki/The_Sorrows_of_Satan "The Sorrows of Satan")* by [Marie Corelli](/wiki/Marie_Corelli "Marie Corelli"), so he produced some drawings for the [title cards](/wiki/Title_cards "Title cards") and sent his work to the studio.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor199624-53) They hired him, and in 1919 he began working for [Islington Studios](/wiki/Islington_Studios "Islington Studios") in Poole Street, [Hoxton](/wiki/Hoxton "Hoxton"), as a title-card designer.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198327-52)

Donald Spoto wrote that most of the staff were Americans with strict job specifications, but the English workers were encouraged to try their hand at anything, which meant that Hitchcock gained experience as a co-writer, art director and production manager on at least 18 silent films.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto20083-54) *The Times* wrote in February 1922 about the studio's "special art title department under the supervision of Mr. A. J. Hitchcock".[[51]](#cite_note-MillerBFI-55) His work included *[Number 13](/wiki/Number_13_(1922_film) "Number 13 (1922 film)")* (1922), also known as *Mrs. Peabody;* it was cancelled because of financial problems - the few finished scenes are [lost](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film")[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto19923-56) – and *[Always Tell Your Wife](/wiki/Always_Tell_Your_Wife "Always Tell Your Wife")* (1923), which he and [Seymour Hicks](/wiki/Seymour_Hicks "Seymour Hicks") finished together when Hicks was about to give up on it.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198327-52) Hicks wrote later about being helped by "a fat youth who was in charge of the property room ... [n]one other than Alfred Hitchcock".[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKerzoncufBarr201545-57)

#### Gainsborough Pictures and work in Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Gainsborough Pictures and work in Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Hitchcock_sculpture%2C_London%2C_2007.jpg/250px-Hitchcock_sculpture%2C_London%2C_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitchcock_sculpture,_London,_2007.jpg)

Hitchcock sculpture at the site of [Gainsborough Pictures](/wiki/Gainsborough_Pictures "Gainsborough Pictures"), Poole Street, [Hoxton](/wiki/Hoxton "Hoxton"), north London[[54]](#cite_note-58)

When Paramount pulled out of London in 1922, Hitchcock was hired as an assistant director by a new firm run in the same location by [Michael Balcon](/wiki/Michael_Balcon "Michael Balcon"), later known as [Gainsborough Pictures](/wiki/Gainsborough_Pictures "Gainsborough Pictures").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198327-52)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto20083–4-59) Hitchcock worked on *[Woman to Woman](/wiki/Woman_to_Woman_(1923_film) "Woman to Woman (1923 film)")* (1923) with the director [Graham Cutts](/wiki/Graham_Cutts "Graham Cutts"), designing the set, writing the script and producing. He said: "It was the first film that I had really got my hands onto."[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto20083–4-59) The editor and "script girl" on *Woman to Woman* was [Alma Reville](/wiki/Alma_Reville "Alma Reville"), his future wife. He also worked as an assistant to Cutts on *[The White Shadow](/wiki/The_White_Shadow_(film) "The White Shadow (film)")* (1924), *[The Passionate Adventure](/wiki/The_Passionate_Adventure "The Passionate Adventure")* (1924), *[The Blackguard](/wiki/The_Blackguard "The Blackguard")* (1925) and *[The Prude's Fall](/wiki/The_Prude%27s_Fall "The Prude's Fall")* (1925).[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198330-60) *The Blackguard* was produced at the [Babelsberg Studios](/wiki/Babelsberg_Studios "Babelsberg Studios") in Potsdam, where Hitchcock watched part of the making of [F. W. Murnau](/wiki/F._W._Murnau "F. W. Murnau")'s *[The Last Laugh](/wiki/The_Last_Laugh_(1924_film) "The Last Laugh (1924 film)")* (1924).[[57]](#cite_note-61) He was impressed with Murnau's work, and later used many of his techniques for the set design in his own productions.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGottlieb200242–43-62)

In the summer of 1925, Balcon asked Hitchcock to direct *[The Pleasure Garden](/wiki/The_Pleasure_Garden_(1925_film) "The Pleasure Garden (1925 film)")* (1926), starring [Virginia Valli](/wiki/Virginia_Valli "Virginia Valli"), a co-production of Gainsborough and the German firm [Emelka](/wiki/Emelka "Emelka") at the [Geiselgasteig studio](/wiki/Bavaria_Studios "Bavaria Studios") near Munich. Reville, by then Hitchcock's fiancée, was assistant director-editor.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198331,_36-63)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto19923-56) Although the film was a commercial flop,[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200368–71-64) Balcon liked Hitchcock's work; a *Daily Express* headline called him the "Young man with a master mind".[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198339-65) On an early sign of his high profile, the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") (BFI) would write, "In an unusual display of confidence for a first time director, Hitchcock included his own signature in the titles."[[62]](#cite_note-66) In March 1926, the British film magazine *[Picturegoer](/wiki/Picturegoer "Picturegoer")* ran an article entitled "Alfred the Great" by the film critic [Cedric Belfrage](/wiki/Cedric_Belfrage "Cedric Belfrage"), who praised Hitchcock for possessing "such a complete grasp of all the different branches of film technique that he is able to take far more control of his production than the average director of four times his experience."[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto199984-67) Production of *The Pleasure Garden* encountered obstacles which Hitchcock would later learn from: on arrival to [Brenner Pass](/wiki/Brenner_Pass "Brenner Pass"), he failed to declare his [film stock](/wiki/Film_stock "Film stock") to customs and it was confiscated; one actress could not enter the water for a scene because she was on her [period](/wiki/Menstrual_cycle "Menstrual cycle"); budget overruns meant that he had to borrow money from the actors.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200370-68) Hitchcock also needed a translator to give instructions to the cast and crew.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200370-68)

In Germany, Hitchcock observed the nuances of [German cinema](/wiki/German_cinema "German cinema") and filmmaking which had a big influence on him.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200363-69) When he was not working, he would visit Berlin's art galleries, concerts and museums. He would also meet with actors, writers and producers to build connections.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200364-70) Balcon asked him to direct a second film in Munich, *[The Mountain Eagle](/wiki/The_Mountain_Eagle "The Mountain Eagle")* (1926), based on an original story titled *Fear o' God*.[[67]](#cite_note-71) The film is lost, and Hitchcock called it "a very bad movie".[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198339-65)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto19925-72) A year later, Hitchcock wrote and directed *[The Ring](/wiki/The_Ring_(1927_film) "The Ring (1927 film)")*; although the screenplay was credited solely to his name, [Elliot Stannard](/wiki/Eliot_Stannard "Eliot Stannard") assisted him with the writing.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200398-73) *The Ring* garnered positive reviews; the *Bioscope* critic called it "the most magnificent British film ever made".[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor199676-74)

When he returned to England, Hitchcock was one of the early members of the London Film Society, newly formed in 1925.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200375-75) Through the Society, he became fascinated by the work by Soviet filmmakers: [Dziga Vertov](/wiki/Dziga_Vertov "Dziga Vertov"), [Lev Kuleshov](/wiki/Lev_Kuleshov "Lev Kuleshov"), [Sergei Eisenstein](/wiki/Sergei_Eisenstein "Sergei Eisenstein") and [Vsevolod Pudovkin](/wiki/Vsevolod_Pudovkin "Vsevolod Pudovkin"). He would also socialise with fellow English filmmakers [Ivor Montagu](/wiki/Ivor_Montagu "Ivor Montagu"), [Adrian Brunel](/wiki/Adrian_Brunel "Adrian Brunel") and [Walter Mycroft](/wiki/Walter_Mycroft "Walter Mycroft").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200376-76) Hitchcock recognised the value in cultivating his own brand, with the director aggressively promoting himself during this period.[[73]](#cite_note-77) In a 1925 London Film Society meeting he declared directors were what mattered most in making films, with [Donald Spoto](/wiki/Donald_Spoto "Donald Spoto") writing that Hitchcock proclaimed, "*We* make a film succeed. The name of the director should be associated in the public's mind with a quality product. Actors come and go, but the name of the director should stay clearly in the mind of the audience."[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto199973-78)

> Visually, it was extraordinarily imaginative for the time, most notably in the scene in which Hitchcock installed a glass floor so that he could show the lodger pacing up and down in his room from below, as though overheard by his landlady.

– [BFI](/wiki/BFI "BFI") entry for Hitchcock's first thriller, *[The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog](/wiki/The_Lodger:_A_Story_of_the_London_Fog "The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog")* (1927)[[75]](#cite_note-79)

Hitchcock established himself as a name director with his first thriller, *[The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog](/wiki/The_Lodger:_A_Story_of_the_London_Fog "The Lodger: A Story of the London Fog")* (1927).[[76]](#cite_note-Lodger_BFI-80) The film concerns the hunt for a [Jack the Ripper](/wiki/Jack_the_Ripper "Jack the Ripper")-style serial killer who, wearing a black cloak and carrying a black bag, is murdering young blonde women in London, and only on Tuesdays.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198345-81) A landlady suspects that her lodger is the killer, but he turns out to be innocent.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198347-82) Hitchcock had wanted the leading man to be guilty, or for the film at least to end ambiguously, but the star was [Ivor Novello](/wiki/Ivor_Novello "Ivor Novello"), a [matinée idol](/wiki/Matin%C3%A9e_idol "Matinée idol"), and the "[star system](/wiki/Star_system_(filmmaking) "Star system (filmmaking)")" meant that Novello could not be the villain. Hitchcock told Truffaut: "You have to clearly spell it out in big letters: 'He is innocent.'" (He had the same problem years later with [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant") in *[Suspicion](#Suspicion)* (1941).)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198343-83) Released in January 1927, *The Lodger* was a commercial and critical success in the UK.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200385-84)[[81]](#cite_note-85) Upon its release, the trade journal *Bioscope* wrote: "It is possible that this film is the finest British production ever made".[[76]](#cite_note-Lodger_BFI-80) Hitchcock told Truffaut that the film was the first of his to be influenced by [German Expressionism](/wiki/German_expressionist_cinema "German expressionist cinema"): "In truth, you might almost say that *The Lodger* was my first picture."[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198344-86) In a strategy for self-publicity, *The Lodger* saw him make his first [cameo appearance](/wiki/List_of_cameo_appearances_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of cameo appearances by Alfred Hitchcock") in a film, where he sat in a newsroom.[[83]](#cite_note-87)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198349-88)

Continuing to market his brand following the success of *The Lodger*, Hitchcock wrote a letter to the *[London Evening News](/wiki/The_Evening_News_(London_newspaper) "The Evening News (London newspaper)")* in November 1927 about his filmmaking, participated in studio-produced publicity, and by December 1927 he developed the original sketch of his widely recognised profile which he introduced by sending it to friends and colleagues as a Christmas present.[[85]](#cite_note-89)

#### Marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Marriage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Hitch_Gets_Hitched.jpg/250px-Hitch_Gets_Hitched.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitch_Gets_Hitched.jpg)

Hitchcock and Reville on their wedding day, [Brompton Oratory](/wiki/Brompton_Oratory "Brompton Oratory"), 2 December 1926

On 2 December 1926, Hitchcock married the English screenwriter [Alma Reville](/wiki/Alma_Reville "Alma Reville") at the [Brompton Oratory](/wiki/Brompton_Oratory "Brompton Oratory") in [South Kensington](/wiki/South_Kensington "South Kensington").[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto19995-90) The couple honeymooned in Paris, [Lake Como](/wiki/Lake_Como "Lake Como") and St. Moritz, before returning to London to live in a leased flat on the top two floors of 153 [Cromwell Road](/wiki/Cromwell_Road "Cromwell Road"), Kensington.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200389–90-91) Reville, who was born just hours after Hitchcock,[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchcock_O'ConnellBouzereau200315-92) converted from Protestantism to Catholicism, apparently at the insistence of Hitchcock's mother; she was baptised on 31 May 1927 and confirmed at [Westminster Cathedral](/wiki/Westminster_Cathedral "Westminster Cathedral") by Cardinal [Francis Bourne](/wiki/Francis_Bourne "Francis Bourne") on 5 June.[[89]](#cite_note-93)

In 1928, when they learned that Reville was pregnant, the Hitchcocks purchased "Winter's Grace", a [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_architecture "Tudor architecture") farmhouse set in eleven acres on Stroud Lane, [Shamley Green](/wiki/Shamley_Green "Shamley Green"), Surrey, for £2,500.[[90]](#cite_note-94) Their daughter and only child, [Patricia (Pat) Alma Hitchcock](/wiki/Pat_Hitchcock "Pat Hitchcock"), was born on 7 July that year.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchcock_O'ConnellBouzereau200359–60-95) Pat died on 9 August 2021 at the age of 93.[[92]](#cite_note-96)

Reville became her husband's closest collaborator; [Charles Champlin](/wiki/Charles_Champlin "Charles Champlin") wrote in 1982: "The Hitchcock touch had four hands, and two were Alma's."[[93]](#cite_note-97) When Hitchcock accepted the [AFI Life Achievement Award](/wiki/AFI_Life_Achievement_Award "AFI Life Achievement Award") in 1979, he said that he wanted to mention "four people who have given me the most affection, appreciation and encouragement, and constant collaboration. The first of the four is a film editor, the second is a scriptwriter, the third is the mother of my daughter, Pat, and the fourth is as fine a cook as ever performed miracles in a domestic kitchen. And their names are Alma Reville."[[94]](#cite_note-98) Reville wrote or co-wrote on many of Hitchcock's films, including *[Shadow of a Doubt](/wiki/Shadow_of_a_Doubt "Shadow of a Doubt")*, *[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")* and *[The 39 Steps](/wiki/The_39_Steps_(1935_film) "The 39 Steps (1935 film)")*.[[95]](#cite_note-99)

#### Early sound films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early sound films")]

Hitchcock began work on his tenth film, *[Blackmail](/wiki/Blackmail_(1929_film) "Blackmail (1929 film)")* (1929), when its production company, [British International Pictures](/wiki/British_International_Pictures "British International Pictures") (BIP), converted its [Elstree studios](/wiki/Elstree_Studios_(Shenley_Road) "Elstree Studios (Shenley Road)") to [sound](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film"). The film was the first British "[talkie](/wiki/Sound_film#Transition:_Europe "Sound film")"; this followed the rapid development of sound films in the United States, from the use of brief sound segments in *[The Jazz Singer](/wiki/The_Jazz_Singer "The Jazz Singer")* (1927) to the first full sound feature *[Lights of New York](/wiki/Lights_of_New_York_(1928_film) "Lights of New York (1928 film)")* (1928).[[4]](#cite_note-Blackmail-5) *Blackmail* began the Hitchcock tradition of using famous landmarks as a backdrop for suspense sequences, which includes an early example of a [red telephone box](/wiki/Red_telephone_box "Red telephone box") being used for criminal activity, while the climax takes place on the dome of the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum").[[96]](#cite_note-Time_Out-100) It also features one of his longest cameo appearances, which shows him being bothered by a small boy as he reads a book on the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground").[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalker200588-101) In the [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") series *The Men Who Made The Movies*, Hitchcock explained how he used early sound recording as a special element of the film to create tension, with a gossipy woman ([Phyllis Monkman](/wiki/Phyllis_Monkman "Phyllis Monkman")) stressing the word "knife" in her conversation with the woman suspected of murder.[[98]](#cite_note-102) During this period, Hitchcock directed segments for a BIP [revue](/wiki/Revue "Revue"), *[Elstree Calling](/wiki/Elstree_Calling "Elstree Calling")* (1930), and directed a short film, *[An Elastic Affair](/wiki/An_Elastic_Affair "An Elastic Affair")* (1930), featuring two *Film Weekly* scholarship winners.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003137-103) *An Elastic Affair* is one of the lost films.[[100]](#cite_note-Kerzoncuf2009-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/The_39_Steps_Still.jpg/250px-The_39_Steps_Still.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_39_Steps_Still.jpg)

[Madeleine Carroll](/wiki/Madeleine_Carroll "Madeleine Carroll") (the archetypal ["Hitchcock blonde"](/wiki/Blonde_stereotype#Typology "Blonde stereotype")) and [Robert Donat](/wiki/Robert_Donat "Robert Donat") in *[The 39 Steps](/wiki/The_39_Steps_(1935_film) "The 39 Steps (1935 film)")* (1935)

In 1933, Hitchcock signed a multi-film contract with [Gaumont-British](/wiki/Gaumont-British "Gaumont-British"), once again working for Michael Balcon.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto199937-105)[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003153-106) His first film for the company, *[The Man Who Knew Too Much](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Knew_Too_Much_(1934_film) "The Man Who Knew Too Much (1934 film)")* (1934), was a success; his second, *[The 39 Steps](/wiki/The_39_Steps_(1935_film) "The 39 Steps (1935 film)")* (1935), was acclaimed in the UK, and gained him recognition in the US. The latter starred the quintessential English "Hitchcock blonde", [Madeleine Carroll](/wiki/Madeleine_Carroll "Madeleine Carroll"), who was the template for his succession of ice-cold, elegant leading ladies.[[103]](#cite_note-Chapman-107) Screenwriter [Robert Towne](/wiki/Robert_Towne "Robert Towne") remarked: "It's not much of an exaggeration to say that all contemporary escapist entertainment begins with *The 39 Steps*".[[104]](#cite_note-108) [John Buchan](/wiki/John_Buchan "John Buchan"), author of *[The Thirty-Nine Steps](/wiki/The_Thirty-Nine_Steps "The Thirty-Nine Steps")* on which the film is loosely based, met with Hitchcock on set, and attended the high-profile premiere at the [New Gallery Cinema](/wiki/New_Gallery_(London) "New Gallery (London)") in London. Upon viewing the film, the author said it had improved on the book.[[103]](#cite_note-Chapman-107) This film was one of the first to introduce the "[MacGuffin](/wiki/MacGuffin "MacGuffin")" plot device, a term coined by the English screenwriter and Hitchcock collaborator [Angus MacPhail](/wiki/Angus_MacPhail "Angus MacPhail").[[105]](#cite_note-109) The MacGuffin is an item or goal the protagonist is pursuing, one that otherwise has no narrative value; in *The 39 Steps*, the MacGuffin is a stolen set of design plans.[[106]](#cite_note-110)

Hitchcock released two spy thrillers in 1936. *[Sabotage](/wiki/Sabotage_(1936_film) "Sabotage (1936 film)")* was loosely based on [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad")'s novel, *[The Secret Agent](/wiki/The_Secret_Agent "The Secret Agent")* (1907), about a woman who discovers that her husband is a terrorist, and *[Secret Agent](/wiki/Secret_Agent_(1936_film) "Secret Agent (1936 film)")*, based on two stories in *[Ashenden: Or the British Agent](/wiki/Ashenden:_Or_the_British_Agent "Ashenden: Or the British Agent")* (1928) by [W. Somerset Maugham](/wiki/W._Somerset_Maugham "W. Somerset Maugham").[[e]](#cite_note-111) In his positive review of *Sabotage* for *[The Spectator](/wiki/The_Spectator "The Spectator")*, the writer and journalist [Graham Greene](/wiki/Graham_Greene "Graham Greene") identified the children's matinée scene as an "ingenious and pathetic twist [stamped as Mr Hitchcock's own](/wiki/Hitchcockian "Hitchcockian")".[[107]](#cite_note-112) *Secret Agent* starred Madeleine Carroll and [John Gielgud](/wiki/John_Gielgud "John Gielgud"), with [Peter Lorre](/wiki/Peter_Lorre "Peter Lorre") playing Gielgud's deranged assistant, and typical Hitchcockian themes include [mistaken identity](/wiki/Eyewitness_identification#Causes_of_eyewitness_error "Eyewitness identification"), trains and a "Hitchcock blonde".[[108]](#cite_note-113)

At this time, Hitchcock also became notorious for pranks against the cast and crew. These jokes ranged from simple and innocent to crazy and maniacal. For instance, he hosted a dinner party where he dyed all the food blue because he claimed there weren't enough blue foods. He also had a horse delivered to the dressing room of his friend, actor [Gerald du Maurier](/wiki/Gerald_du_Maurier "Gerald du Maurier").[[109]](#cite_note-114) Hitchcock's next film, *[Young and Innocent](/wiki/Young_and_Innocent "Young and Innocent")* (1937), was a crime thriller based on the 1936 novel *[A Shilling for Candles](/wiki/A_Shilling_for_Candles "A Shilling for Candles")* by [Josephine Tey](/wiki/Josephine_Tey "Josephine Tey").[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996137-115) Starring [Nova Pilbeam](/wiki/Nova_Pilbeam "Nova Pilbeam") and [Derrick De Marney](/wiki/Derrick_De_Marney "Derrick De Marney"), the film was relatively enjoyable for the cast and crew to make.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996137-115) To meet distribution purposes in America, the film's runtime was cut and this included removal of one of Hitchcock's favourite scenes: a children's tea party which becomes menacing to the protagonists.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996138-116)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/The-Lady-Vanishes-1938.jpg/250px-The-Lady-Vanishes-1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:The-Lady-Vanishes-1938.jpg)

[Margaret Lockwood](/wiki/Margaret_Lockwood "Margaret Lockwood") (middle) and [Michael Redgrave](/wiki/Michael_Redgrave "Michael Redgrave") (right) in a publicity shot for *[The Lady Vanishes](/wiki/The_Lady_Vanishes "The Lady Vanishes")* (1938)

Hitchcock's next major success was *[The Lady Vanishes](/wiki/The_Lady_Vanishes "The Lady Vanishes")* (1938), "one of the greatest train movies from the genre's golden era", according to [Philip French](/wiki/Philip_French "Philip French"), in which Miss Froy ([May Whitty](/wiki/May_Whitty "May Whitty")), a British spy posing as a governess, disappears on a train journey through the [fictional European country of Bandrika](/wiki/List_of_fictional_countries_on_the_Earth "List of fictional countries on the Earth").[[112]](#cite_note-117) The film saw Hitchcock receive the [1938 New York Film Critics Circle Award](/wiki/1938_New_York_Film_Critics_Circle_Awards "1938 New York Film Critics Circle Awards") for Best Director.[[113]](#cite_note-118) Benjamin Crisler of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote in June 1938: "Three unique and valuable institutions the British have that we in America have not: [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"), the [Tower Bridge](/wiki/Tower_Bridge "Tower Bridge") and Alfred Hitchcock, the greatest director of screen melodramas in the world."[[114]](#cite_note-119) The film was based on the novel *[The Wheel Spins](/wiki/The_Wheel_Spins "The Wheel Spins")* (1936) written by [Ethel Lina White](/wiki/Ethel_Lina_White "Ethel Lina White"), and starred [Michael Redgrave](/wiki/Michael_Redgrave "Michael Redgrave") (in his film debut) and [Margaret Lockwood](/wiki/Margaret_Lockwood "Margaret Lockwood").[[115]](#cite_note-120)[[116]](#cite_note-121)

By 1938, Hitchcock was aware that he had reached his peak in Britain.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996140-122) He had received numerous offers from producers in the United States, but he turned them all down because he disliked the contractual obligations or thought the projects were repellent.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996141-123) However, producer [David O. Selznick](/wiki/David_O._Selznick "David O. Selznick") offered him a concrete proposal to make a film based on the sinking of [RMS *Titanic*](/wiki/Titanic "Titanic"), which was eventually shelved, but Selznick persuaded Hitchcock to come to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"). In June 1938, Hitchcock sailed to New York aboard the [RMS *Queen Mary*](/wiki/RMS_Queen_Mary "RMS Queen Mary"),[[119]](#cite_note-:1-124) and found that he was already a celebrity; he was featured in magazines and gave interviews to radio stations.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996142-125) In Hollywood, Hitchcock met Selznick for the first time. Selznick offered him a four-film contract, approximately $40,000 for each picture (equivalent to $910,000 in 2025).[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996142-125) Before finalising any American deal, Hitchcock had one last film to make in England, as director of the [Charles Laughton](/wiki/Charles_Laughton "Charles Laughton")-produced picture *[Jamaica Inn](/wiki/Jamaica_Inn_(film) "Jamaica Inn (film)")* (1939), which he had signed on to make in May 1938, right before his first trip to the US.[[119]](#cite_note-:1-124)

### Early Hollywood years: 1939–1945

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Early Hollywood years: 1939–1945")]

#### Selznick contract

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Selznick contract")]

Selznick signed Hitchcock to a seven-year contract beginning in April 1939,[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983121-126) and the Hitchcocks moved to Hollywood.[[122]](#cite_note-127) The Hitchcocks lived in a spacious flat on [Wilshire Boulevard](/wiki/Wilshire_Boulevard "Wilshire Boulevard"), and slowly acclimatised themselves to the Los Angeles area. He and his wife Alma kept a low profile, and were not interested in attending parties or being celebrities.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996153-128) Hitchcock discovered his taste for fine food in West Hollywood, but still carried on his way of life from England.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996154-129) He was impressed with Hollywood's filmmaking culture, expansive budgets and efficiency,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996154-129) compared to the limits that he had often faced in Britain.[[125]](#cite_note-130) In June that year, *[Life](/wiki/Life_(magazine) "Life (magazine)")* called him the "greatest master of melodrama in screen history".[[126]](#cite_note-131)

Although Hitchcock and Selznick respected each other, their working arrangements were sometimes difficult. Selznick suffered from constant financial problems, and Hitchcock was often unhappy about Selznick's creative control and interference over his films. Selznick was also displeased with Hitchcock's method of shooting just what was in the script, and nothing more, which meant that the film could not be cut and remade differently at a later time.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996152-132) As well as complaining about Hitchcock's "goddamn jigsaw cutting",[[128]](#cite_note-133) their personalities were mismatched: Hitchcock was reserved whereas Selznick was flamboyant.[[129]](#cite_note-134) Eventually, Selznick generously lent Hitchcock to the larger film studios.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983145-135) Selznick made only a few films each year, as did fellow independent producer [Samuel Goldwyn](/wiki/Samuel_Goldwyn "Samuel Goldwyn"), so he did not always have projects for Hitchcock to direct. Goldwyn had also negotiated with Hitchcock on a possible contract, only to be outbid by Selznick. In a later interview, Hitchcock said: "[Selznick] was the Big Producer. ... Producer was king. The most flattering thing Mr. Selznick ever said about me—and it shows you the amount of control—he said I was the 'only director' he'd 'trust with a film'."[[131]](#cite_note-136)

Trailer for *Rebecca* (1940)

Hitchcock approached American cinema cautiously; his first American film was set in England in which the "Americanness" of the characters was incidental:[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood2002240-137) *[Rebecca](/wiki/Rebecca_(1940_film) "Rebecca (1940 film)")* (1940) was set in a Hollywood version of England's Cornwall and based on a [novel](/wiki/Rebecca_(novel) "Rebecca (novel)") by English novelist [Daphne du Maurier](/wiki/Daphne_du_Maurier "Daphne du Maurier"). Selznick insisted on a faithful adaptation of the book, and disagreed with Hitchcock with the use of humour.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996150-138)[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996155-139) The film, starring [Laurence Olivier](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier "Laurence Olivier") and [Joan Fontaine](/wiki/Joan_Fontaine "Joan Fontaine"), concerns an unnamed naïve young woman who marries a widowed aristocrat. She lives in his large [English country house](/wiki/English_country_house "English country house"), and struggles with the lingering reputation of his elegant and worldly first wife Rebecca, who died under mysterious circumstances. The film won [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture") at the [13th Academy Awards](/wiki/13th_Academy_Awards "13th Academy Awards"); the statuette was given to producer Selznick. Hitchcock received his first nomination for [Best Director](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Director "Academy Award for Best Director"), his first of five such nominations.[[5]](#cite_note-Rebecca-6)[[135]](#cite_note-140)

Hitchcock's second American film was the thriller *[Foreign Correspondent](/wiki/Foreign_Correspondent_(film) "Foreign Correspondent (film)")* (1940), set in Europe, based on [Vincent Sheean](/wiki/Vincent_Sheean "Vincent Sheean")'s book *Personal History* (1935) and produced by [Walter Wanger](/wiki/Walter_Wanger "Walter Wanger"). It was nominated for Best Picture that year. Hitchcock felt uneasy living and working in Hollywood while Britain was at war; his concern resulted in a film that overtly supported the British war effort.[[136]](#cite_note-141) Filmed in 1939, it was inspired by the rapidly changing events in Europe, as covered by an American newspaper reporter played by [Joel McCrea](/wiki/Joel_McCrea "Joel McCrea"). By mixing footage of European scenes with scenes filmed on a Hollywood [backlot](/wiki/Backlot "Backlot"), the film avoided direct references to [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism"), [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and Germans, to comply with the [Motion Picture Production Code](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Production_Code "Motion Picture Production Code") at the time.[[137]](#cite_note-142)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

#### Early war years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Early war years")]

In September 1940, the Hitchcocks bought the 200-acre (0.81 km2) Cornwall Ranch near [Scotts Valley](/wiki/Scotts_Valley "Scotts Valley"), California, in the [Santa Cruz Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_Mountains "Santa Cruz Mountains").[[138]](#cite_note-143) Their primary residence was an English-style home in [Bel Air](/wiki/Bel_Air,_Los_Angeles "Bel Air, Los Angeles"), purchased in 1942.[[139]](#cite_note-Variety_obituary-144) Hitchcock's films were diverse during this period, ranging from the romantic comedy *[Mr. & Mrs. Smith](/wiki/Mr._%26_Mrs._Smith_(1941_film) "Mr. & Mrs. Smith (1941 film)")* (1941) to the bleak [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir") *[Shadow of a Doubt](/wiki/Shadow_of_a_Doubt "Shadow of a Doubt")* (1943).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Cary_Grant_Joan_Fontaine_Suspicion.jpg/250px-Cary_Grant_Joan_Fontaine_Suspicion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cary_Grant_Joan_Fontaine_Suspicion.jpg)

[Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant") and [Joan Fontaine](/wiki/Joan_Fontaine "Joan Fontaine") in a publicity shot for *[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")* (1941)

*[Suspicion](/wiki/Suspicion_(1941_film) "Suspicion (1941 film)")* (1941) marked Hitchcock's first film as a producer and director. It is set in England; Hitchcock used the north coast of [Santa Cruz](/wiki/Santa_Cruz,_California "Santa Cruz, California") for the English coastline sequence. The film is the first of four in which [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant") was cast by Hitchcock, and it is one of the rare occasions that Grant plays a sinister character. Grant plays Johnnie Aysgarth, an English [conman](/wiki/Conman "Conman") whose actions raise suspicion and anxiety in his shy young English wife, Lina McLaidlaw ([Joan Fontaine](/wiki/Joan_Fontaine "Joan Fontaine")).[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitty2016434–435-145) In one scene, Hitchcock placed a light inside a glass of milk, perhaps poisoned, that Grant is bringing to his wife; the light ensures that the audience's attention is on the glass. Grant's character is actually a killer, according to the book, *[Before the Fact](/wiki/Before_the_Fact "Before the Fact")* by [Francis Iles](/wiki/Francis_Iles "Francis Iles"), but the studio felt that Grant's image would be tarnished by that. Hitchcock would have preferred to end with the wife's murder.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983142–143-146)[[f]](#cite_note-148) Instead, the actions that she found suspicious are a reflection of his own despair and his plan to commit suicide. Fontaine won [Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") for her performance.[[143]](#cite_note-149)

*[Saboteur](/wiki/Saboteur_(film) "Saboteur (film)")* (1942) is the first of two films that Hitchcock made for [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") during the decade. Hitchcock wanted [Gary Cooper](/wiki/Gary_Cooper "Gary Cooper") and [Barbara Stanwyck](/wiki/Barbara_Stanwyck "Barbara Stanwyck") or [Henry Fonda](/wiki/Henry_Fonda "Henry Fonda") and [Gene Tierney](/wiki/Gene_Tierney "Gene Tierney") to star, but was forced by Universal to use Universal contract player [Robert Cummings](/wiki/Robert_Cummings "Robert Cummings") and [Priscilla Lane](/wiki/Priscilla_Lane "Priscilla Lane"), a freelancer who signed a one-picture deal with the studio, both known for their work in comedies and light dramas.[[144]](#cite_note-150) The story depicts a confrontation between a suspected saboteur (Cummings) and a real saboteur ([Norman Lloyd](/wiki/Norman_Lloyd "Norman Lloyd")) atop the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"). Hitchcock took a three-day tour of New York City to scout for *Saboteur*'s filming locations.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003445-151) He also directed *Have You Heard?* (1942), a photographic dramatisation for *[Life](/wiki/Life_(magazine) "Life (magazine)")* magazine of the [dangers of rumours during wartime](/wiki/Loose_lips_sink_ships "Loose lips sink ships").[[146]](#cite_note-152) In 1943, he wrote a mystery story for *[Look](/wiki/Look_(American_magazine) "Look (American magazine)")*, "The Murder of [Monty Woolley](/wiki/Monty_Woolley "Monty Woolley")",[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrunsdale2010442-153) a sequence of captioned photographs inviting the reader to find clues to the murderer's identity; Hitchcock cast the performers as themselves, such as Woolley, Doris Merrick and make-up man Guy Pearce.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*[Shadow of a Doubt](/wiki/Shadow_of_a_Doubt "Shadow of a Doubt")* (1943) trailer with [Joseph Cotten](/wiki/Joseph_Cotten "Joseph Cotten") and [Teresa Wright](/wiki/Teresa_Wright "Teresa Wright")

Back in England, Hitchcock's mother Emma was severely ill; she died on 26 September 1942 at age 79. Hitchcock never spoke publicly about his mother, but his assistant said that he admired her.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003321-154) Four months later, on 4 January 1943, his brother William died of an overdose at age 52.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003325-155) Hitchcock was not very close to William,[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996193-156) but his death made Hitchcock conscious about his own eating and drinking habits. He was overweight and suffering from back aches. His New Year's resolution in 1943 was to take his diet seriously with the help of a physician.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003326-157) In January that year, *[Shadow of a Doubt](/wiki/Shadow_of_a_Doubt "Shadow of a Doubt")* was released, which Hitchcock had fond memories of making.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003327-158) In the film, Charlotte "Charlie" Newton ([Teresa Wright](/wiki/Teresa_Wright "Teresa Wright")) suspects her beloved uncle Charlie Oakley ([Joseph Cotten](/wiki/Joseph_Cotten "Joseph Cotten")) of being a serial killer. Hitchcock filmed extensively on location, this time in the Northern California city of [Santa Rosa](/wiki/Santa_Rosa,_California "Santa Rosa, California").[[153]](#cite_note-159)

He told Truffaut in 1962:

> At the time, I was on a strenuous diet, painfully working my way from three hundred to two hundred pounds. So I decided to immortalize my loss and get my bit part by posing for "before" and "after" pictures. ... I was literally submerged by letters from fat people who wanted to know where and how they could get Reduco.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983158–159-160)

Hitchcock's typical dinner before his weight loss had been a roast chicken, boiled ham, potatoes, bread, vegetables, relishes, salad, dessert, a bottle of wine and some brandy. To lose weight, his diet consisted of black coffee for breakfast and lunch, and steak and salad for dinner,[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003326-157) but it was hard to maintain; Donald Spoto wrote that his weight fluctuated considerably over the next 40 years. At the end of 1943, despite the weight loss, the Occidental Insurance Company of Los Angeles refused his application for life insurance.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto1999266–267-161)

#### Wartime non-fiction films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Wartime non-fiction films")]

Further information: [German Concentration Camps Factual Survey](/wiki/German_Concentration_Camps_Factual_Survey "German Concentration Camps Factual Survey")

> I felt the need to make a little contribution to the war effort, and I was both overweight and over-age for military service. I knew that if I did nothing, I'd regret it for the rest of my life

– Alfred Hitchcock (1967)[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983159-162)

Hitchcock returned to the UK for an extended visit in late 1943 and early 1944. While there he made two short [propaganda films](/wiki/Propaganda_film "Propaganda film"), *[Bon Voyage](/wiki/Bon_Voyage_(1944_film) "Bon Voyage (1944 film)")* (1944) and *[Aventure Malgache](/wiki/Aventure_Malgache "Aventure Malgache")* (1944), for the [Ministry of Information](/wiki/Ministry_of_Information_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Information (United Kingdom)"). In June and July 1945, Hitchcock served as "treatment advisor" on a [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") documentary that used [Allied Forces](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") footage of the liberation of [Nazi concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps"). The film was assembled in London and produced by [Sidney Bernstein](/wiki/Sidney_Bernstein,_Baron_Bernstein "Sidney Bernstein, Baron Bernstein") of the Ministry of Information, who brought Hitchcock (a friend of his) on board. It was originally intended to be broadcast to the Germans, but the British government deemed it too traumatic to be shown to a shocked post-war population. Instead, it was transferred in 1952 from the [British War Office](/wiki/British_War_Office "British War Office") film vaults to London's [Imperial War Museum](/wiki/Imperial_War_Museum "Imperial War Museum") and remained unreleased until 1985, when an edited version was broadcast as an episode of [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") *[Frontline](/wiki/Frontline_(U.S._TV_series) "Frontline (U.S. TV series)")*, under the title the Imperial War Museum had given it: *Memory of the Camps*. The full-length version of the film, *[German Concentration Camps Factual Survey](/wiki/German_Concentration_Camps_Factual_Survey "German Concentration Camps Factual Survey")*, was restored in 2014 by scholars at the Imperial War Museum.[[157]](#cite_note-163)[[158]](#cite_note-Jeffries9Jan2015-164)[[159]](#cite_note-165)

### Post-war Hollywood years: 1945–1953

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Post-war Hollywood years: 1945–1953")]

#### Later Selznick films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Later Selznick films")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Spellbound-1945.jpg/250px-Spellbound-1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spellbound-1945.jpg)

[Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") and [Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman") in *[Spellbound](/wiki/Spellbound_(1945_film) "Spellbound (1945 film)")* (1945)

Hitchcock worked for David Selznick again when he directed *[Spellbound](/wiki/Spellbound_(1945_film) "Spellbound (1945 film)")* (1945), which explores [psychoanalysis](/wiki/Psychoanalysis "Psychoanalysis") and features a [dream sequence](/wiki/Dream_sequence "Dream sequence") designed by [Salvador Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí").[[160]](#cite_note-166) The dream sequence as it appears in the film is ten minutes shorter than was originally envisioned; Selznick edited it to make it "play" more effectively.[[161]](#cite_note-167) [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") plays amnesiac Dr. Anthony Edwardes under the treatment of analyst Dr. Peterson ([Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman")), who falls in love with him while trying to unlock his repressed past.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhitty2016408–412-168) Two [point-of-view](/wiki/Point-of-view_shot "Point-of-view shot") shots were achieved by building a large wooden hand (which would appear to belong to the character whose point of view the camera took) and out-sized props for it to hold: a bucket-sized glass of milk and a large wooden gun. For added novelty and impact, the climactic gunshot was hand-coloured red on some copies of the black-and-white film. The original musical score by [Miklós Rózsa](/wiki/Mikl%C3%B3s_R%C3%B3zsa "Miklós Rózsa") makes use of the [theremin](/wiki/Theremin "Theremin"), and some of it was later adapted by the composer into Rozsa's Piano Concerto Op. 31 (1967) for piano and orchestra.[[163]](#cite_note-169)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The spy film *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(1946_film) "Notorious (1946 film)")* followed next in 1946. Hitchcock told François Truffaut that Selznick sold him, Ingrid Bergman, Cary Grant and [Ben Hecht](/wiki/Ben_Hecht "Ben Hecht")'s screenplay, to [RKO Radio Pictures](/wiki/RKO_Radio_Pictures "RKO Radio Pictures") as a "package" for $500,000 (equivalent to $8.3 million in 2025) because of cost overruns on Selznick's *[Duel in the Sun](/wiki/Duel_in_the_Sun_(film) "Duel in the Sun (film)")* (1946).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *Notorious* stars Bergman and Grant, both Hitchcock collaborators, and features a plot about Nazis, [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium") and South America. His prescient use of uranium as a plot device led to him being briefly placed under surveillance by the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation").[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983168-170) According to Patrick McGilligan, in or around March 1945, Hitchcock and Hecht consulted [Robert Millikan](/wiki/Robert_Millikan "Robert Millikan") of the [California Institute of Technology](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology") about the development of a uranium bomb. Selznick complained that the notion was "science fiction", only to be confronted by the news of the detonation of two atomic bombs on [Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Hiroshima and Nagasaki") in Japan in August 1945.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003370–371-171)

#### Transatlantic Pictures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transatlantic Pictures")]

[![A typical scene from Rope showing James Stewart](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Ropetrailer2.jpg/250px-Ropetrailer2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ropetrailer2.jpg)

A typical shot from *[Rope](/wiki/Rope_(film) "Rope (film)")* (1948) with [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart") turning his back to the fixed camera

Hitchcock formed an independent production company, [Transatlantic Pictures](/wiki/Transatlantic_Pictures "Transatlantic Pictures"), with his friend [Sidney Bernstein](/wiki/Sidney_Bernstein,_Baron_Bernstein "Sidney Bernstein, Baron Bernstein"). He made two films with Transatlantic, one of which was his first colour film. With *[Rope](/wiki/Rope_(film) "Rope (film)")* (1948), Hitchcock experimented with marshalling suspense in a confined environment, as he had done earlier with *Lifeboat*. The film appears as a very limited number of continuous shots, but it was actually shot in 10 ranging from 4+1⁄2 to 10 minutes each; a 10-minute length of film was the most that a camera's film magazine could hold at the time. Some transitions between reels were hidden by having a dark object fill the entire screen for a moment. Hitchcock used those points to hide the cut, and began the next take with the camera in the same place. The film features [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart") in the leading role, and was the first of four films that Stewart made with Hitchcock. It was inspired by the [Leopold and Loeb](/wiki/Leopold_and_Loeb "Leopold and Loeb") case of the 1920s.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003421-172) Critical response at the time was mixed.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2004-173)

*[Under Capricorn](/wiki/Under_Capricorn "Under Capricorn")* (1949), set in 19th-century Australia, also uses the short-lived technique of long takes, but to a more limited extent. He again used [Technicolor](/wiki/Technicolor "Technicolor") in this production, then returned to [black-and-white](/wiki/Black-and-white "Black-and-white") for several years. Transatlantic Pictures became inactive after the last two films.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto1999138-174)[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996179-175) Hitchcock filmed *[Stage Fright](/wiki/Stage_Fright_(1950_film) "Stage Fright (1950 film)")* (1950) at [Elstree Studios](/wiki/Elstree_Studios_(Shenley_Road) "Elstree Studios (Shenley Road)") in England, where he had worked during his British International Pictures contract many years before.[[170]](#cite_note-176) He paired one of [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Early_history_of_Warner_Bros._Pictures "Early history of Warner Bros. Pictures")' most popular stars, [Jane Wyman](/wiki/Jane_Wyman "Jane Wyman"), with the expatriate German actor [Marlene Dietrich](/wiki/Marlene_Dietrich "Marlene Dietrich") and used several prominent British actors, including [Michael Wilding](/wiki/Michael_Wilding "Michael Wilding"), [Richard Todd](/wiki/Richard_Todd "Richard Todd") and [Alastair Sim](/wiki/Alastair_Sim "Alastair Sim").[[171]](#cite_note-177) This was Hitchcock's first proper production for Warner Bros., which had distributed *Rope* and *Under Capricorn*, because Transatlantic Pictures was experiencing financial difficulties.[[172]](#cite_note-178)

His thriller *[Strangers on a Train](/wiki/Strangers_on_a_Train_(film) "Strangers on a Train (film)")* (1951) was based on the [novel of the same name](/wiki/Strangers_on_a_Train_(novel) "Strangers on a Train (novel)") by [Patricia Highsmith](/wiki/Patricia_Highsmith "Patricia Highsmith"). Hitchcock combined many elements from his preceding films. He approached [Dashiell Hammett](/wiki/Dashiell_Hammett "Dashiell Hammett") to write the dialogue, but [Raymond Chandler](/wiki/Raymond_Chandler "Raymond Chandler") took over, then left over disagreements with the director. In the film, two men casually meet, one of whom speculates on a foolproof method to murder; he suggests that two people, each wishing to do away with someone, should each perform the other's murder. [Farley Granger](/wiki/Farley_Granger "Farley Granger")'s role was as the innocent victim of the scheme, while [Robert Walker](/wiki/Robert_Walker_(actor,_born_1918) "Robert Walker (actor, born 1918)"), previously known for "boy-next-door" roles, played the villain.[[173]](#cite_note-179) *[I Confess](/wiki/I_Confess_(film) "I Confess (film)")* (1953) was set in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") with [Montgomery Clift](/wiki/Montgomery_Clift "Montgomery Clift") as a Catholic priest.[[174]](#cite_note-180)

### Peak years: 1954–1964

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Peak years: 1954–1964")]

#### *Dial M for Murder* and *Rear Window*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Dial M for Murder and Rear Window")]

[![Still image from the film Read Window featuring Stewart and Kelly](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Rearwindow_trailer_2.jpg/250px-Rearwindow_trailer_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rearwindow_trailer_2.jpg)

[James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart") and [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly") in *[Rear Window](/wiki/Rear_Window "Rear Window")* (1954)

*I Confess* was followed by three colour films starring [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly"): *[Dial M for Murder](/wiki/Dial_M_for_Murder "Dial M for Murder")* (1954), *[Rear Window](/wiki/Rear_Window "Rear Window")* (1954) and *[To Catch a Thief](/wiki/To_Catch_a_Thief "To Catch a Thief")* (1955). In *Dial M for Murder*, [Ray Milland](/wiki/Ray_Milland "Ray Milland") plays the villain who tries to murder his unfaithful wife (Kelly) for her money. She kills the hired assassin in self-defence, so Milland manipulates the evidence to make it look like murder. Her lover, Mark Halliday ([Robert Cummings](/wiki/Robert_Cummings "Robert Cummings")), and Police Inspector Hubbard ([John Williams](/wiki/John_Williams_(actor) "John Williams (actor)")) save her from execution.[[175]](#cite_note-Leitch_2002._p._78-80-181) Hitchcock experimented with [3D cinematography](/wiki/3-D_film "3-D film") for *Dial M for Murder*.[[176]](#cite_note-182)

Hitchcock moved to [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") and filmed *[Rear Window](/wiki/Rear_Window "Rear Window")* (1954), starring James Stewart and Grace Kelly, as well as [Thelma Ritter](/wiki/Thelma_Ritter "Thelma Ritter") and [Raymond Burr](/wiki/Raymond_Burr "Raymond Burr"). Stewart's character is a photographer named Jeff (based on [Robert Capa](/wiki/Robert_Capa "Robert Capa")) who must temporarily use a wheelchair. Out of boredom, he begins observing his neighbours across the courtyard, then becomes convinced that one of them (Raymond Burr) has murdered his wife. Jeff eventually manages to convince his policeman buddy ([Wendell Corey](/wiki/Wendell_Corey "Wendell Corey")) and his girlfriend (Kelly). As with *Lifeboat* and *Rope*, the principal characters are depicted in confined or cramped quarters, in this case Stewart's studio apartment. Hitchcock uses close-ups of Stewart's face to show his character's reactions, "from the comic voyeurism directed at his neighbours to his helpless terror watching Kelly and Burr in the villain's apartment".[[177]](#cite_note-183)

#### *Alfred Hitchcock Presents*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Alfred Hitchcock Presents")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Alfred_Hitchcock_and_family_circa_1955.JPG/250px-Alfred_Hitchcock_and_family_circa_1955.JPG)](/wiki/File:Alfred_Hitchcock_and_family_circa_1955.JPG)

The Hitchcocks with their [daughter](/wiki/Pat_Hitchcock "Pat Hitchcock"), son-in-law, and granddaughters, c. 1955–1956

From 1955 to 1965, Hitchcock was the host of the television series *[Alfred Hitchcock Presents](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents "Alfred Hitchcock Presents")*.[[178]](#cite_note-Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents-184) With his droll delivery, gallows humour, and distinctive features, the series made Hitchcock a celebrity. The title-sequence of the show pictured a minimalist caricature of his profile (he drew it himself; it is composed of only nine strokes), which his real silhouette then filled.[[179]](#cite_note-185) The series theme tune was *[Funeral March of a Marionette](/wiki/Funeral_March_of_a_Marionette "Funeral March of a Marionette")* by the French composer [Charles Gounod](/wiki/Charles_Gounod "Charles Gounod") (1818–1893).[[180]](#cite_note-186)

His introductions always included some sort of wry humour, such as the description of a recent multi-person execution hampered by having only one [electric chair](/wiki/Electric_chair "Electric chair"), while two are shown with a sign "Two chairs—no waiting!" He directed 18 episodes of the series, which aired from 1955 to 1965. It became *[The Alfred Hitchcock Hour](/wiki/The_Alfred_Hitchcock_Hour "The Alfred Hitchcock Hour")* in 1962, and NBC broadcast the final episode on 10 May 1965. In the 1980s, a [new version](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents_(1985_TV_series) "Alfred Hitchcock Presents (1985 TV series)") of *Alfred Hitchcock Presents* was produced for television, making use of Hitchcock's original introductions in a [colourised](/wiki/Colourised "Colourised") form.[[178]](#cite_note-Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents-184)

Hitchcock's success in television spawned a set of short-story collections in his name; these included *[Alfred Hitchcock's Anthology](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock%27s_Anthology "Alfred Hitchcock's Anthology")*, *Stories They Wouldn't Let Me Do on TV,* and *Tales My Mother Never Told Me*.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996202-187) In 1956, HSD Publications also licensed the director's name to create *[Alfred Hitchcock's Mystery Magazine](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock%27s_Mystery_Magazine "Alfred Hitchcock's Mystery Magazine")*, a monthly [digest](/wiki/Digest_size "Digest size") specialising in crime and detective fiction.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996202-187) Hitchcock's television series were very profitable, and his foreign-language versions of books were bringing revenues of up to $100,000 a year (equivalent to $1,090,000 in 2025).[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996203-188)

#### From *To Catch a Thief* to *Vertigo*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: From To Catch a Thief to Vertigo")]

In 1955, Hitchcock became a United States citizen.[[183]](#cite_note-189) In the same year, his third Grace Kelly film, *[To Catch a Thief](/wiki/To_Catch_a_Thief "To Catch a Thief")*, was released; it is set in the [French Riviera](/wiki/French_Riviera "French Riviera"), and stars Kelly and Cary Grant. Grant plays retired thief John Robie, who becomes the prime suspect for a spate of robberies in the Riviera. A thrill-seeking American heiress played by Kelly surmises his true identity and tries to seduce him. "Despite the obvious age disparity between Grant and Kelly and a lightweight plot, the witty script (loaded with double entendres) and the good-natured acting proved a commercial success."[[184]](#cite_note-Leitch_2002._p._366-190) It was Hitchcock's last film with Kelly; she married [Prince Rainier](/wiki/Prince_Rainier "Prince Rainier") of Monaco in 1956, and ended her film career afterward. Hitchcock then remade his own [1934 film](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Knew_Too_Much_(1934_film) "The Man Who Knew Too Much (1934 film)") *The Man Who Knew Too Much* [in 1956](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Knew_Too_Much_(1956_film) "The Man Who Knew Too Much (1956 film)"). This time, the film starred James Stewart and [Doris Day](/wiki/Doris_Day "Doris Day"), who sang the theme song "[Que Sera, Sera](/wiki/Que_Sera,_Sera "Que Sera, Sera")", which won the [Academy Award for Best Original Song](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Original_Song "Academy Award for Best Original Song") and became a big hit. They play a couple whose son is kidnapped to prevent them from interfering with an assassination. As in the 1934 film, the climax takes place at the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall").[[185]](#cite_note-191)

*[The Wrong Man](/wiki/The_Wrong_Man "The Wrong Man")* (1956), Hitchcock's final film for Warner Bros., is a low-key black-and-white production based on a real-life case of [mistaken identity](/wiki/Mistaken_identity_(error) "Mistaken identity (error)") reported in *Life* magazine in 1953. This was the only film of Hitchcock to star [Henry Fonda](/wiki/Henry_Fonda "Henry Fonda"), playing a [Stork Club](/wiki/Stork_Club "Stork Club") musician mistaken for a liquor store thief, who is arrested and tried for robbery while his wife ([Vera Miles](/wiki/Vera_Miles "Vera Miles")) emotionally collapses under the strain. Hitchcock told Truffaut that his lifelong fear of the police attracted him to the subject and was embedded in many scenes.[[186]](#cite_note-Leitch_2002._p._377-192)

[![Still image from the film Vertigo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Vertigo_1958_trailer_Kim_Novak_at_Golden_Gate_Bridge_Fort_Point.jpg/250px-Vertigo_1958_trailer_Kim_Novak_at_Golden_Gate_Bridge_Fort_Point.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vertigo_1958_trailer_Kim_Novak_at_Golden_Gate_Bridge_Fort_Point.jpg)

[Kim Novak](/wiki/Kim_Novak "Kim Novak") by the [Golden Gate Bridge](/wiki/Golden_Gate_Bridge "Golden Gate Bridge") in *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* (1958)[[g]](#cite_note-193)

While directing episodes for *Alfred Hitchcock Presents* during the summer of 1957, Hitchcock was admitted to hospital for [hernia](/wiki/Hernia "Hernia") and [gallstones](/wiki/Gallstone "Gallstone"), and had to have his [gallbladder](/wiki/Gallbladder "Gallbladder") removed. Following a successful surgery, he immediately returned to work to prepare for his next project.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996212-194)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2004-173) *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* (1958) again starred James Stewart, with [Kim Novak](/wiki/Kim_Novak "Kim Novak") and [Barbara Bel Geddes](/wiki/Barbara_Bel_Geddes "Barbara Bel Geddes"). He had wanted [Vera Miles](/wiki/Vera_Miles "Vera Miles") to play the lead, but she was pregnant. He told [Oriana Fallaci](/wiki/Oriana_Fallaci "Oriana Fallaci"): "I was offering her a big part, the chance to become a beautiful sophisticated blonde, a real actress. We'd have spent a heap of dollars on it, and she has the bad taste to get pregnant. I hate pregnant women, because then they have children."[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFallaci1963-195)

In *Vertigo*, Stewart plays Scottie, a former police investigator suffering from [acrophobia](/wiki/Acrophobia "Acrophobia"), who becomes obsessed with a woman he has been hired to shadow (Novak). Scottie's obsession leads to tragedy, and this time Hitchcock did not opt for a happy ending.
