Some critics, including Donald Spoto and [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert"), agree that *Vertigo* is the director's most personal and revealing film, dealing with the *[Pygmalion](/wiki/Pygmalion_(mythology) "Pygmalion (mythology)")*-like obsessions of a man who moulds a woman into the person he desires. *Vertigo* explores more frankly and at greater length his interest in the relation between sex and death, than any other work in his filmography.[[189]](#cite_note-196)

*Vertigo* contains a camera technique developed by Irmin Roberts, commonly referred to as a [dolly zoom](/wiki/Dolly_zoom "Dolly zoom"), which has been copied by many filmmakers. The film premiered at the [San Sebastián International Film Festival](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n_International_Film_Festival "San Sebastián International Film Festival"), and Hitchcock won the Silver Seashell prize.[[190]](#cite_note-197) *Vertigo* is considered a classic, but it attracted mixed reviews and poor box-office receipts at the time;[[191]](#cite_note-198) the critic from *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* opined that the film was "too slow and too long".[[192]](#cite_note-199) [Bosley Crowther](/wiki/Bosley_Crowther "Bosley Crowther") of the *New York Times* thought it was "devilishly far-fetched", but praised the cast performances and Hitchcock's direction.[[193]](#cite_note-200) The picture was also the last collaboration between Stewart and Hitchcock.[[194]](#cite_note-Leitch_2002._p._376-77-201) In the 2002 *[Sight & Sound](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound "Sight & Sound")* polls, it ranked just behind *[Citizen Kane](/wiki/Citizen_Kane "Citizen Kane")* (1941); ten years later, in the same magazine, critics chose it as the best film ever made.[[7]](#cite_note-Christie2012-8)

#### *North by Northwest* and *Psycho*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: North by Northwest and Psycho")]

See also: [Psycho (franchise)](/wiki/Psycho_(franchise) "Psycho (franchise)")

After *Vertigo*, the rest of 1958 was a difficult year for Hitchcock. During [pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production") of *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")* (1959), which was a "slow" and "agonising" process, his wife Alma was diagnosed with cancer.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996217-202) While she was in hospital, Hitchcock kept himself occupied with his television work and would visit her every day. Alma underwent surgery and made a full recovery, but it caused Hitchcock to imagine, for the first time, life without her.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996217-202)

[![Photo of Alfred Hitchcock & Janet Leigh from the 1960 film Psycho](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Hitchcock_Leigh_Psycho.jpg/250px-Hitchcock_Leigh_Psycho.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitchcock_Leigh_Psycho.jpg)

Hitchcock shooting the shower scene of *Psycho* (1960)

Hitchcock followed up with three more successful films, which are also recognised as among his best: *North by Northwest*, *[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)")* (1960) and *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(film) "The Birds (film)")* (1963). In *North by Northwest*, Cary Grant portrays Roger Thornhill, a [Madison Avenue](/wiki/Madison_Avenue "Madison Avenue") advertising executive who is mistaken for a government secret agent. He is pursued across the United States by enemy agents, including Eve Kendall ([Eva Marie Saint](/wiki/Eva_Marie_Saint "Eva Marie Saint")). At first, Thornhill believes Kendall is helping him, but then realises that she is an enemy agent; he later learns that she is working undercover for the [CIA](/wiki/CIA "CIA"). During its opening two-week run at [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall"), the film grossed $404,056 (equivalent to $4.5 million in 2025), setting a non-holiday gross record for that theatre.[[196]](#cite_note-203) *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine called the film "smoothly troweled and thoroughly entertaining".[[197]](#cite_note-204)

*[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)")* (1960) is arguably Hitchcock's best-known film.[[198]](#cite_note-Leitch_2002._p._260-205) Based on [Robert Bloch](/wiki/Robert_Bloch "Robert Bloch")'s 1959 novel *[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(novel) "Psycho (novel)")*, which was inspired by the case of [Ed Gein](/wiki/Ed_Gein "Ed Gein"),[[199]](#cite_note-206) the film was produced on a tight budget of $800,000 (equivalent to $8.7 million in 2025) and shot in black-and-white on a spare set using crew members from *[Alfred Hitchcock Presents](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock_Presents "Alfred Hitchcock Presents")*.[[200]](#cite_note-207) The unprecedented violence of the shower scene,[[h]](#cite_note-209) the early death of the heroine, and the innocent lives extinguished by a disturbed murderer became the hallmarks of a new horror-film genre.[[202]](#cite_note-210) The film proved popular with audiences, with lines stretching outside theatres as viewers waited for the next showing. It broke box-office records in the United Kingdom, France, South America, the United States and Canada, and was a moderate success in Australia for a brief period.[[203]](#cite_note-Leigh_1995-211)

*Psycho* was the most profitable of Hitchcock's career, and he personally earned in excess of $15 million (equivalent to $160 million in 2025). He subsequently swapped his rights to *Psycho* and his TV anthology for 150,000 shares of [MCA](/wiki/MCA_Inc. "MCA Inc."), making him the third largest shareholder and his own boss at Universal, in theory at least, although that did not stop studio interference.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERebello1990182-212) Following the first film, *Psycho* became an American horror [franchise](/wiki/Media_franchise "Media franchise"): *[Psycho II](/wiki/Psycho_II_(film) "Psycho II (film)")*, *[Psycho III](/wiki/Psycho_III "Psycho III")*, *[Bates Motel](/wiki/Bates_Motel_(film) "Bates Motel (film)")*, *[Psycho IV: The Beginning](/wiki/Psycho_IV:_The_Beginning "Psycho IV: The Beginning")* and a colour [1998 remake](/wiki/Psycho_(1998_film) "Psycho (1998 film)") of the original.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVerevis200622-213)

#### Truffaut interview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Truffaut interview")]

Further information: [Hitchcock/Truffaut](/wiki/Hitchcock/Truffaut "Hitchcock/Truffaut") and [Hitchcock/Truffaut (film)](/wiki/Hitchcock/Truffaut_(film) "Hitchcock/Truffaut (film)")

On 13 August 1962, Hitchcock's 63rd birthday, the French director [François Truffaut](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Truffaut "François Truffaut") began a 50-hour interview of Hitchcock, filmed over eight days at Universal Studios, during which Hitchcock agreed to answer 500 questions. It took four years to transcribe the tapes and organise the images; it was published as a book in 1967, which Truffaut nicknamed the "Hitchbook". The audio tapes were used as the basis of a documentary in 2015.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198312-214)[[207]](#cite_note-215) Truffaut sought the interview because it was clear to him that Hitchcock was not simply the mass-market entertainer the American media made him out to be. It was obvious from his films, Truffaut wrote, that Hitchcock had "given more thought to the potential of his art than any of his colleagues". He compared the interview to "Oedipus' consultation of the oracle".[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198311–12-216)

#### *The Birds*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: The Birds")]

Further information: [Tippi Hedren § Sexual harassment](/wiki/Tippi_Hedren#Sexual_harassment "Tippi Hedren")

Trailer for *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(film) "The Birds (film)")* (1963), in which Hitchcock discusses humanity's treatment of "our feathered friends"

The film scholar Peter William Evans wrote that *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(film) "The Birds (film)")* (1963) and *[Marnie](/wiki/Marnie_(film) "Marnie (film)")* (1964) are regarded as "undisputed masterpieces".[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2004-173) Hitchcock had intended to film *Marnie* first, and in March 1962 it was announced that Grace Kelly, Princess Grace of Monaco since 1956, would come out of retirement to star in it.[[209]](#cite_note-217) When Kelly asked Hitchcock to postpone *Marnie* until 1963 or 1964, he recruited [Evan Hunter](/wiki/Evan_Hunter "Evan Hunter"), author of *The Blackboard Jungle* (1954), to develop a screenplay based on a [Daphne du Maurier](/wiki/Daphne_du_Maurier "Daphne du Maurier") short story, "[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(story) "The Birds (story)")" (1952), which Hitchcock had republished in his *My Favorites in Suspense* (1959). He hired [Tippi Hedren](/wiki/Tippi_Hedren "Tippi Hedren") to play the lead role.[[210]](#cite_note-218) It was her first role; she had been a model in New York when Hitchcock saw her, in October 1961, in an NBC television advert for [Sego](/wiki/Sego_(diet_drink) "Sego (diet drink)"), a diet drink:[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral201315-219) "I signed her because she is a classic beauty. Movies don't have them any more. Grace Kelly was the last." He insisted, without explanation, that her first name be written in single quotation marks: 'Tippi'.[[i]](#cite_note-222)

In *The Birds*, Melanie Daniels, a young socialite, meets lawyer Mitch Brenner ([Rod Taylor](/wiki/Rod_Taylor "Rod Taylor")) in a bird shop; [Jessica Tandy](/wiki/Jessica_Tandy "Jessica Tandy") plays his possessive mother. Hedren visits him in [Bodega Bay](/wiki/Bodega_Bay,_California "Bodega Bay, California") (where *The Birds* was filmed)[[212]](#cite_note-McDonald1April1962-220) carrying a pair of [lovebirds](/wiki/Lovebird "Lovebird") as a gift. Suddenly waves of birds start gathering, watching, and attacking. The question: "What do the birds want?" is left unanswered.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERothman2014203-223) Hitchcock made the film with equipment from the Revue Studio, which made *Alfred Hitchcock Presents*. He said it was his most technically challenging film, using a combination of trained and mechanical birds against a backdrop of wild ones. Every shot was sketched in advance.[[212]](#cite_note-McDonald1April1962-220)

An [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO")/[BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") television film, *[The Girl](/wiki/The_Girl_(2012_TV_film) "The Girl (2012 TV film)")* (2012), depicted Hedren's experiences on set; she said that Hitchcock became obsessed with her and sexually harassed her. He reportedly isolated her from the rest of the crew, had her followed, whispered obscenities to her, had her handwriting analysed and had a ramp built from his private office directly into her trailer.[[215]](#cite_note-224)[[216]](#cite_note-Goldman5October2012-225) [Diane Baker](/wiki/Diane_Baker "Diane Baker"), her co-star in *Marnie*, said: "[N]othing could have been more horrible for me than to arrive on that movie set and to see her being treated the way she was."[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto2008265-226) While filming the attack scene in the attic – which took a week to film – she was placed in a caged room while two men wearing [elbow-length protective gloves](/wiki/Evening_glove "Evening glove") threw live birds at her. Toward the end of the week, to stop the birds' flying away from her too soon, one leg of each bird was attached by nylon thread to elastic bands sewn inside her clothes. She broke down after a bird cut her lower eyelid, and filming was halted on doctor's orders.[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto1999457–459-227)

#### *Marnie*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Marnie")]

Trailer for *[Marnie](/wiki/Marnie_(film) "Marnie (film)")* (1964)

In June 1962, Grace Kelly announced that she had decided against appearing in *[Marnie](/wiki/Marnie_(film) "Marnie (film)")* (1964). Hedren had signed an exclusive seven-year, $500-a-week contract with Hitchcock in October 1961,[[219]](#cite_note-228) and he decided to cast her in the lead role opposite [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery"). In 2016, describing Hedren's performance as "one of the greatest in the history of cinema", [Richard Brody](/wiki/Richard_Brody "Richard Brody") called the film a "story of sexual violence" inflicted on the character played by Hedren: "The film is, to put it simply, sick, and it's so because Hitchcock was sick. He suffered all his life from furious sexual desire, suffered from the lack of its gratification, suffered from the inability to transform fantasy into reality, and then went ahead and did so virtually, by way of his art."[[220]](#cite_note-Brody17Aug2016-229) A 1964 *New York Times* review called it Hitchcock's "most disappointing film in years", citing Hedren's and Connery's lack of experience, an amateurish script and "glaringly fake cardboard backdrops".[[221]](#cite_note-230)

In the film, Marnie Edgar (Hedren) steals $40,000 from her employer and goes on the run. She applies for a job at Mark Rutland's (Connery) company in Philadelphia and steals from there too. Earlier, she is shown having a panic attack during a thunderstorm and fearing the colour red. Mark tracks her down and blackmails her into marrying him. She explains that she does not want to be touched, but during the "honeymoon", Mark rapes her. Marnie and Mark discover that Marnie's mother had been a prostitute when Marnie was a child, and that, while the mother was fighting with a client during a thunderstorm – the mother believed the client had tried to molest Marnie – Marnie had killed the client to save her mother. When she remembers what happened, she decides to stay with Mark.[[220]](#cite_note-Brody17Aug2016-229)[[222]](#cite_note-Cleaver13Aug2012-231)

Hitchcock told cinematographer [Robert Burks](/wiki/Robert_Burks "Robert Burks") that the camera had to be placed as close as possible to Hedren when he filmed her face.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto1999471-232) [Evan Hunter](/wiki/Evan_Hunter "Evan Hunter"), the screenwriter of *The Birds* who was writing *Marnie* too, explained to Hitchcock that, if Mark loved Marnie, he would comfort her, not rape her. Hitchcock reportedly replied: "Evan, when he sticks it in her, I want that camera right on her face!"[[224]](#cite_note-233) When Hunter submitted two versions of the script, one without the rape scene, Hitchcock replaced him with [Jay Presson Allen](/wiki/Jay_Presson_Allen "Jay Presson Allen").[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral201338–39-234)

### Later years: 1966–1980

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Later years: 1966–1980")]

#### Final films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Final films")]

Failing health reduced Hitchcock's output during the last two decades of his life. Biographer [Stephen Rebello](/wiki/Stephen_Rebello "Stephen Rebello") claimed Universal imposed two films on him, *[Torn Curtain](/wiki/Torn_Curtain "Torn Curtain")* (1966) and *[Topaz](/wiki/Topaz_(1969_film) "Topaz (1969 film)")* (1969), the latter of which is based on a [Leon Uris](/wiki/Leon_Uris "Leon Uris") novel, partly set in Cuba.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERebello1990188-235) Both were spy thrillers with [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War")-related themes. *Torn Curtain*, with [Paul Newman](/wiki/Paul_Newman "Paul Newman") and [Julie Andrews](/wiki/Julie_Andrews "Julie Andrews"), precipitated the bitter end of the twelve-year collaboration between Hitchcock and composer [Bernard Herrmann](/wiki/Bernard_Herrmann "Bernard Herrmann").[[227]](#cite_note-236) Hitchcock was unhappy with Herrmann's score and replaced him with [John Addison](/wiki/John_Addison "John Addison"), [Jay Livingston](/wiki/Jay_Livingston "Jay Livingston") and [Ray Evans](/wiki/Ray_Evans "Ray Evans").[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith2002273–274-237) Upon release, *Torn Curtain* was a box office disappointment,[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983328-238) and *Topaz* was disliked by both critics and the studio.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983333-239)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Alfred_Hitchcock_and_Karen_Black.jpg/250px-Alfred_Hitchcock_and_Karen_Black.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfred_Hitchcock_and_Karen_Black.jpg)

Hitchcock with [Karen Black](/wiki/Karen_Black "Karen Black") during a press junket for *[Family Plot](/wiki/Family_Plot "Family Plot")* (1976)

Hitchcock returned to Britain to make his penultimate film, *[Frenzy](/wiki/Frenzy "Frenzy")* (1972), based on the novel *[Goodbye Piccadilly, Farewell Leicester Square](/wiki/Goodbye_Piccadilly,_Farewell_Leicester_Square "Goodbye Piccadilly, Farewell Leicester Square")* (1966). After two espionage films, the plot marked a return to the murder-thriller genre. Richard Blaney ([Jon Finch](/wiki/Jon_Finch "Jon Finch")), a volatile barman with a history of explosive anger, becomes the prime suspect in the investigation into the "Necktie Murders", which are actually committed by his friend Bob Rusk ([Barry Foster](/wiki/Barry_Foster_(actor) "Barry Foster (actor)")). This time, Hitchcock makes the victim and villain kindreds, rather than opposites, as in *Strangers on a Train*.[[231]](#cite_note-240)

In *Frenzy*, Hitchcock allowed nudity for the first time. Two scenes show naked women, one of whom is being raped and strangled;[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2004-173) Donald Spoto called the latter "one of the most repellent examples of a detailed murder in the history of film". Both actors, [Barbara Leigh-Hunt](/wiki/Barbara_Leigh-Hunt "Barbara Leigh-Hunt") and [Anna Massey](/wiki/Anna_Massey "Anna Massey"), refused to do the scenes, so models were used instead.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto1999513–514-241) Biographers have noted that Hitchcock had always pushed the limits of film censorship, often managing to fool [Joseph Breen](/wiki/Joseph_Breen "Joseph Breen"), the head of the [Motion Picture Production Code](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Production_Code "Motion Picture Production Code"). Hitchcock would add subtle hints of improprieties forbidden by censorship until the mid-1960s. Yet, Patrick McGilligan wrote that Breen and others often realised that Hitchcock was inserting such material and were actually amused, as well as alarmed by Hitchcock's "inescapable inferences".[[233]](#cite_note-242)

*[Family Plot](/wiki/Family_Plot "Family Plot")* (1976) was Hitchcock's last film. It relates the escapades of "Madam" Blanche Tyler, played by [Barbara Harris](/wiki/Barbara_Harris_(actress) "Barbara Harris (actress)"), a fraudulent spiritualist, and her taxi-driver lover [Bruce Dern](/wiki/Bruce_Dern "Bruce Dern"), making a living from her phony powers. While *Family Plot* was based on the [Victor Canning](/wiki/Victor_Canning "Victor Canning") novel *[The Rainbird Pattern](/wiki/The_Rainbird_Pattern "The Rainbird Pattern")* (1972), the novel's tone is more sinister. Screenwriter [Ernest Lehman](/wiki/Ernest_Lehman "Ernest Lehman") originally wrote the film, under the working title *Deception*, with a dark tone but was pushed to a lighter, more comical tone by Hitchcock where it took the name *Deceit*, then finally, *Family Plot*.[[234]](#cite_note-243)

#### Knighthood and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Knighthood and death")]

Toward the end of his life, Hitchcock was working on the script for a spy thriller, *[The Short Night](/wiki/The_Short_Night "The Short Night")*, collaborating with [James Costigan](/wiki/James_Costigan "James Costigan"), [Ernest Lehman](/wiki/Ernest_Lehman "Ernest Lehman") and [David Freeman](/wiki/David_Freeman_(screenwriter) "David Freeman (screenwriter)"). Despite preliminary work, it was never filmed. Hitchcock's health was declining and he was worried about his wife, who had suffered a [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"). The screenplay was eventually published in Freeman's book *The Last Days of Alfred Hitchcock* (1999).[[235]](#cite_note-244)

Having refused a [CBE](/wiki/CBE "CBE") in 1962,[[236]](#cite_note-245) Hitchcock was appointed a [Knight Commander of the Most Excellent Order of the British Empire](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") (KBE) in the [1980 New Year Honours](/wiki/1980_New_Year_Honours "1980 New Year Honours").[[10]](#cite_note-Todd30April1980-13)[[237]](#cite_note-246) He was too ill to travel to London—he had a [pacemaker](/wiki/Pacemaker "Pacemaker") and was being given [cortisone](/wiki/Cortisone "Cortisone") injections for his arthritis—so on 3 January 1980 the British consul general presented him with the papers at Universal Studios. Asked by a reporter after the ceremony why it had taken the Queen so long, Hitchcock quipped, "I suppose it was a matter of carelessness." Cary Grant, Janet Leigh and others attended a luncheon afterwards.[[238]](#cite_note-Spoto_1999_553-247)[[239]](#cite_note-Ebert2Jan1980-248)

His last public appearance was on 16 March 1980, when he introduced the next year's winner of the American Film Institute award.[[238]](#cite_note-Spoto_1999_553-247) He died of [kidney failure](/wiki/Kidney_failure "Kidney failure") the following month, on 29 April, at his [Bel Air](/wiki/Bel_Air,_Los_Angeles "Bel Air, Los Angeles") home.[[139]](#cite_note-Variety_obituary-144)[[240]](#cite_note-249) [Donald Spoto](/wiki/Donald_Spoto "Donald Spoto"), one of Hitchcock's biographers, wrote that Hitchcock had declined to see a priest,[[241]](#cite_note-250) but according to Jesuit priest Mark Henninger, he and another priest, Tom Sullivan, celebrated Mass at the filmmaker's home, and Sullivan heard his [confession](/wiki/Sacrament_of_Penance "Sacrament of Penance").[[242]](#cite_note-251) Hitchcock was survived by his wife and daughter. His funeral was held at Good Shepherd Catholic Church in Beverly Hills on 30 April, after which his body was [cremated](/wiki/Cremation "Cremation"). His remains were scattered over the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") on 10 May.[[243]](#cite_note-252)[[244]](#cite_note-obit-253)

## Filmmaking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Filmmaking")]

### Style and themes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Style and themes")]

Main articles: [Themes and plot devices in Hitchcock films](/wiki/Themes_and_plot_devices_in_Hitchcock_films "Themes and plot devices in Hitchcock films") and [List of cameo appearances by Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/List_of_cameo_appearances_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of cameo appearances by Alfred Hitchcock")

The "[Hitchcockian](/wiki/Hitchcockian "Hitchcockian")" style includes the use of editing and camera movement to mimic a person's gaze, thereby turning viewers into [voyeurs](/wiki/Voyeurs "Voyeurs"), and framing [shots](/wiki/Shot_(filmmaking) "Shot (filmmaking)") to maximise anxiety and fear. The film critic [Robin Wood](/wiki/Robin_Wood_(critic) "Robin Wood (critic)") wrote that the meaning of a Hitchcock film "is there in the method, in the progression from shot to shot. A Hitchcock film is an organism, with the whole implied in every detail and every detail related to the whole."[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood200262-254)

Hitchcock's film production career evolved from small-scale silent films to financially significant sound films. Hitchcock remarked that he was influenced by early filmmakers [Georges Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès"), D. W. Griffith and [Alice Guy-Blaché](/wiki/Alice_Guy-Blach%C3%A9 "Alice Guy-Blaché").[[246]](#cite_note-255) His silent films between 1925 and 1929 were in the crime and suspense genres, but also included melodramas and comedies. Whilst [visual storytelling](/wiki/Visual_storytelling "Visual storytelling") was pertinent during the silent era, even after the arrival of sound, Hitchcock still relied on visuals in cinema; he referred to this emphasis on visual storytelling as "pure cinema".[[247]](#cite_note-256) In Britain, he honed his craft so that by the time he moved to Hollywood, the director had perfected his style and camera techniques. Hitchcock later said that his British work was the "sensation of cinema", whereas the American phase was when his "ideas were fertilised".[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983123-257) Scholar [Robin Wood](/wiki/Robin_Wood_(critic) "Robin Wood (critic)") writes that the director's first two films, *The Pleasure Garden* and *The Mountain Eagle*, were influenced by [German Expressionism](/wiki/German_expressionist_cinema "German expressionist cinema"). Afterward, he discovered [Soviet cinema](/wiki/Soviet_cinema "Soviet cinema"), and [Sergei Eisenstein](/wiki/Sergei_Eisenstein "Sergei Eisenstein")'s and [Vsevolod Pudovkin](/wiki/Vsevolod_Pudovkin "Vsevolod Pudovkin")'s theories of [montage](/wiki/Soviet_montage_theory "Soviet montage theory").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan200375-75) 1926's *The Lodger* was inspired by both German and Soviet aesthetics, styles which solidified the rest of his career.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood2002207-258) Although Hitchcock's work in the 1920s found some success, several British reviewers criticised Hitchcock's films for being unoriginal and conceited.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESloan199517-259) [Raymond Durgnat](/wiki/Raymond_Durgnat "Raymond Durgnat") opined that Hitchcock's films were carefully and intelligently constructed, but thought they can be shallow and rarely present a "coherent worldview".[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESloan1995400-260)

Earning the title "Master of Suspense", the director experimented with ways to generate tension in his work.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESloan199517-259) He said:

> My suspense work comes out of creating nightmares for the audience. And I *play* with an audience. I make them gasp and surprise them and shock them. When you have a nightmare, it's awfully vivid if you're dreaming that you're being led to the electric chair. Then you're as happy as can be when you wake up because you're relieved.[[252]](#cite_note-:0-261)

During filming of *North by Northwest*, Hitchcock explained his reasons for recreating the set of [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore"): "The audience responds in proportion to how realistic you make it. One of the dramatic reasons for this type of photography is to get it looking so natural that the audience gets involved and believes, for the time being, what's going on up there on the screen."[[252]](#cite_note-:0-261) In a 1963 interview with Italian journalist [Oriana Fallaci](/wiki/Oriana_Fallaci "Oriana Fallaci"), Hitchcock was asked how in spite of appearing to be a pleasant, innocuous man, he seemed to enjoy making films involving suspense and terrifying crime. He responded:

> I'm English. The English use a lot of imagination with their crimes. I don't get such a kick out of anything as much as out of imagining a crime. When I'm writing a story and I come to a crime, I think happily: now wouldn't it be nice to have him die like this? And then, even more happily, I think: at this point people will start yelling. It must be because I spent three years studying with the Jesuits. They used to terrify me to death, with everything, and now I'm getting my own back by terrifying other people.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGottlieb200356-262)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Leytonstone_tube_station_-_Hitchcock_Gallery-_Hitchcock_The_Director_%28geograph_4081878%29.jpg/250px-Leytonstone_tube_station_-_Hitchcock_Gallery-_Hitchcock_The_Director_%28geograph_4081878%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leytonstone_tube_station_-_Hitchcock_Gallery-_Hitchcock_The_Director_(geograph_4081878).jpg)

*Hitchcock The Director* mosaic in the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground")

Hitchcock's films, from the silent to the sound era, contained a number of recurring themes that he is famous for. His films explored the audience as [voyeurs](/wiki/Voyeur "Voyeur"), notably in *Rear Window*, *Marnie* and *Psycho*. He understood that human beings enjoy voyeuristic activities and made the audience participate in it through the character's actions.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983216-263) Of his fifty-three films, eleven revolved around stories of [mistaken identity](/wiki/Mistaken_identity_(error) "Mistaken identity (error)"), where an innocent protagonist is accused of a crime and is pursued by police. In most cases, it is an ordinary, everyday person who finds themselves in a dangerous situation.[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198348-264) Hitchcock told Truffaut: "That's because the theme of the innocent man being accused, I feel, provides the audience with a greater sense of danger. It's easier for them to identify with him than with a guilty man on the run."[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut198348-264) One of his constant themes was the struggle of a personality torn between "order and chaos";[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood200298-265) known as the notion of "double", which is a comparison or contrast between two characters or objects: the double representing a dark or evil side.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2004-173)

According to Robin Wood, Hitchcock retained a feeling of ambivalence towards homosexuality, despite working with gay actors throughout his career.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood2002342-266) Donald Spoto suggests that Hitchcock's [sexually repressive](/wiki/Sexually_repressive "Sexually repressive") childhood may have contributed to his exploration of [deviancy](/wiki/Deviancy "Deviancy").[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood2002342-266) During the 1950s, the [Motion Picture Production Code](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Production_Code "Motion Picture Production Code") prohibited direct references to homosexuality but the director was known for his subtle references,[[258]](#cite_note-267) and pushing the boundaries of the censors. Moreover, *Shadow of a Doubt* has a double [incest](/wiki/Incest "Incest") theme through the storyline, expressed implicitly through images.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood2002300-268) Author Jane Sloan argues that Hitchcock was drawn to both conventional and unconventional sexual expression in his work,[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESloan199516-269) and the theme of marriage was usually presented in a "bleak and skeptical" manner.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWood2002246-270) It was also not until after his mother's death in 1942, that Hitchcock portrayed motherly figures as "notorious monster-mothers".[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003321-154) The [espionage](/wiki/Espionage "Espionage") backdrop, and murders committed by characters with [psychopathic](/wiki/Psychopathic "Psychopathic") tendencies were common themes too.[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGilligan2003128-271) In Hitchcock's depiction of villains and murderers, they were usually charming and friendly, forcing viewers to identify with them.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996293-272) The director's strict childhood and Jesuit education may have led to his distrust of authority figures such as policemen and politicians; a theme which he has explored.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2004-173) Also, he often employed the "[MacGuffin](/wiki/MacGuffin "MacGuffin")": an object essential to the plot but insignificant in itself.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor1996120-273)

Hitchcock appeared briefly in most of his own films. For example, he is seen struggling to get a double bass onto a train (*[Strangers on a Train](/wiki/Strangers_on_a_Train_(film) "Strangers on a Train (film)")*), walking dogs out of a pet shop (*[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(film) "The Birds (film)")*), fixing a neighbour's clock (*[Rear Window](/wiki/Rear_Window "Rear Window")*), as a shadow (*[Family Plot](/wiki/Family_Plot "Family Plot")*), sitting at a table in a photograph (*[Dial M for Murder](/wiki/Dial_M_for_Murder "Dial M for Murder")*), and riding a bus (*[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")*, *[To Catch a Thief](/wiki/To_Catch_a_Thief "To Catch a Thief")*).[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalker200588-101)

### Representation of women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Representation of women")]

Hitchcock's portrayal of women has been the subject of much scholarly debate. [Bidisha](/wiki/Bidisha "Bidisha") wrote in *The Guardian* in 2010: "There's the vamp, the tramp, the snitch, the witch, the slink, the double-crosser and, best of all, the demon mommy. Don't worry, they all get punished in the end."[[265]](#cite_note-274) In a widely cited essay in 1975, [Laura Mulvey](/wiki/Laura_Mulvey "Laura Mulvey") introduced the idea of the [male gaze](/wiki/Male_gaze "Male gaze"); the view of the spectator in Hitchcock's films, she argued, is that of the heterosexual male protagonist.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMulvey1989-275) "The female characters in his films reflected the same qualities over and over again", [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") wrote in 1996: "They were blonde. They were icy and remote. They were imprisoned in costumes that subtly combined fashion with fetishism. They mesmerised the men, who often had physical or psychological handicaps. Sooner or later, every Hitchcock woman was humiliated."[[267]](#cite_note-276)[[j]](#cite_note-278)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Vertigo_1958_trailer_embrace.jpg/250px-Vertigo_1958_trailer_embrace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vertigo_1958_trailer_embrace.jpg)

[Kim Novak](/wiki/Kim_Novak "Kim Novak") and [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart") in *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* (1958)

Hitchcock's films often feature characters struggling in their relationships with their mothers, such as Norman Bates in *Psycho*. In *North by Northwest*, Roger Thornhill ([Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant")) is an innocent man ridiculed by his mother for insisting that shadowy, murderous men are after him. In *The Birds*, the Rod Taylor character, an innocent man, finds his world under attack by vicious birds, and struggles to free himself from a clinging mother ([Jessica Tandy](/wiki/Jessica_Tandy "Jessica Tandy")). The killer in *Frenzy* has a loathing of women but idolises his mother. The villain Bruno in *Strangers on a Train* hates his father, but has an incredibly close relationship with his mother (played by [Marion Lorne](/wiki/Marion_Lorne "Marion Lorne")). Sebastian ([Claude Rains](/wiki/Claude_Rains "Claude Rains")) in *Notorious* has a clearly conflicting relationship with his mother, who is (rightly) suspicious of his new bride, Alicia Huberman ([Ingrid Bergman](/wiki/Ingrid_Bergman "Ingrid Bergman")).[[269]](#cite_note-279)

### Relationship with actors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Relationship with actors")]

> I told her that my idea of a good actor or good actress is someone who can do nothing very well. ... I said, "That's one of the things you've got to learn to have ... authority." Out of authority comes control and out of control you get the range ... Whether you do little acting, a lot of acting in a given scene. You know exactly where you're going. And these were the first things that she had to know. Emotion comes later and the control of the voice comes later. But, within herself, she had to learn authority first and foremost because out of authority comes timing.

– Alfred Hitchcock (1967)[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoral201318-280)

Hitchcock became known for having remarked that "actors should be treated like cattle".[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983140-281)[[k]](#cite_note-282) During the filming of *[Mr. & Mrs. Smith](/wiki/Mr._%26_Mrs._Smith_(1941_film) "Mr. & Mrs. Smith (1941 film)")* (1941), [Carole Lombard](/wiki/Carole_Lombard "Carole Lombard") brought three cows onto the set wearing the name tags of Lombard, [Robert Montgomery](/wiki/Robert_Montgomery_(actor) "Robert Montgomery (actor)"), and [Gene Raymond](/wiki/Gene_Raymond "Gene Raymond"), the stars of the film, to surprise him.[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983140-281) In an episode of *[The Dick Cavett Show](/wiki/The_Dick_Cavett_Show "The Dick Cavett Show")*, originally broadcast on 8 June 1972, [Dick Cavett](/wiki/Dick_Cavett "Dick Cavett") stated as fact that Hitchcock had once called actors cattle. Hitchcock responded by saying that, at one time, he had been accused of calling actors cattle. "I said that I would never say such an unfeeling, rude thing about actors at all. What I probably said, was that all actors should be treated like cattle...In a nice way of course." He then described Carole Lombard's joke, with a smile.[[272]](#cite_note-283)

Hitchcock believed that actors should concentrate on their performances and leave work on script and character to the directors and screenwriters. He told [Bryan Forbes](/wiki/Bryan_Forbes "Bryan Forbes") in 1967: "I remember discussing with a method actor how he was taught and so forth. He said, 'We're taught using improvisation. We are given an idea and then we are turned loose to develop in any way we want to.' I said, 'That's not acting. That's writing.'"[[142]](#cite_note-HitchcockForbes-147)

Recalling their experiences on *Lifeboat* for Charles Chandler, author of *It's Only a Movie: Alfred Hitchcock A Personal Biography,* [Walter Slezak](/wiki/Walter_Slezak "Walter Slezak") said that Hitchcock "knew more about how to help an actor than any director I ever worked with", and [Hume Cronyn](/wiki/Hume_Cronyn "Hume Cronyn") dismissed the idea that Hitchcock was not concerned with his actors as "utterly fallacious", describing at length the process of rehearsing and filming *Lifeboat*.[[273]](#cite_note-284)

Critics observed that, despite his reputation as a man who disliked actors, actors who worked with him often gave brilliant performances. He used the same actors in many of his films; Cary Grant and James Stewart both worked with Hitchcock four times,[[274]](#cite_note-285) and Ingrid Bergman and Grace Kelly three. [James Mason](/wiki/James_Mason "James Mason") said that Hitchcock regarded actors as "animated props".[[275]](#cite_note-286) For Hitchcock, the actors were part of the film's setting. He told François Truffaut: "The chief requisite for an actor is the ability to do nothing well, which is by no means as easy as it sounds. He should be willing to be used and wholly integrated into the picture by the director and the camera. He must allow the camera to determine the proper emphasis and the most effective dramatic highlights."[[276]](#cite_note-287)

### Writing, storyboards and production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Writing, storyboards and production")]

Hitchcock planned his scripts in detail with his writers. In *Writing with Hitchcock* (2001), Steven DeRosa noted that Hitchcock supervised them through every draft, asking that they tell the story visually.[[277]](#cite_note-288) Hitchcock told Roger Ebert in 1969:

> Once the screenplay is finished, I'd just as soon not make the film at all. All the fun is over. I have a strongly visual mind. I visualize a picture right down to the final cuts. I write all this out in the greatest detail in the script, and then I don't look at the script while I'm shooting. I know it off by heart, just as an orchestra conductor needs not look at the score. It's melancholy to shoot a picture. When you finish the script, the film is perfect. But in shooting it you lose perhaps 40 per cent of your original conception.[[278]](#cite_note-289)

Hitchcock's films were extensively [storyboarded](/wiki/Storyboard "Storyboard") to the finest detail. He was reported to have never even bothered looking through the [viewfinder](/wiki/Viewfinder "Viewfinder"), since he did not need to, although in publicity photos he was shown doing so. He also used this as an excuse to never have to change his films from his initial vision. If a studio asked him to change a film, he would claim that it was already shot in a single way, and that there were no alternative takes to consider.[[279]](#cite_note-290)

[![Image of Hitchcock pictured under Mount Rushmore during the filming of North by Northwest](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Alfred_Hitchcock_on_the_set_of_North_By_Northwest.jpg/250px-Alfred_Hitchcock_on_the_set_of_North_By_Northwest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alfred_Hitchcock_on_the_set_of_North_By_Northwest.jpg)

Hitchcock at [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore") filming *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")* (1959)

This view of Hitchcock as a director who relied more on pre-production than on the actual production itself has been challenged by Bill Krohn, the American correspondent of French film magazine *[Cahiers du Cinéma](/wiki/Cahiers_du_Cin%C3%A9ma "Cahiers du Cinéma")*, in his book *Hitchcock at Work*. After investigating script revisions, notes to other production personnel written by or to Hitchcock, and other production material, Krohn observed that Hitchcock's work often deviated from how the screenplay was written or how the film was originally envisioned.[[280]](#cite_note-krohn1–7-291) He noted that the myth of storyboards in relation to Hitchcock, often regurgitated by generations of commentators on his films, was to a great degree perpetuated by Hitchcock himself or the publicity arm of the studios. For example, the celebrated crop-spraying sequence of *North by Northwest* was not storyboarded at all. After the scene was filmed, the publicity department asked Hitchcock to make storyboards to promote the film, and Hitchcock in turn hired an artist to match the scenes in detail.[[281]](#cite_note-Raymond_Bellour_2000_p._217-292)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Even when storyboards were made, scenes that were shot differed from them significantly. Krohn's analysis of the production of Hitchcock classics like *Notorious* reveals that Hitchcock was flexible enough to change a film's conception during its production. Another example Krohn notes is the American remake of *The Man Who Knew Too Much,* whose shooting schedule commenced without a finished script and moreover went over schedule, something that, as Krohn notes, was not an uncommon occurrence on many of Hitchcock's films, including *[Strangers on a Train](/wiki/Strangers_on_a_Train_(film) "Strangers on a Train (film)")* and *[Topaz](/wiki/Topaz_(1969_film) "Topaz (1969 film)")*. While Hitchcock did do a great deal of preparation for all his films, he was fully cognisant that the actual film-making process often deviated from the best-laid plans and was flexible to adapt to the changes and needs of production as his films were not free from the normal hassles faced and common routines used during many other film productions.[[281]](#cite_note-Raymond_Bellour_2000_p._217-292)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Hitchcock interview, c. 1966

Krohn's work also sheds light on Hitchcock's practice of generally shooting in chronological order, which he notes sent many films over budget and over schedule and, more importantly, differed from the standard operating procedure of Hollywood in the [studio system](/wiki/Studio_system "Studio system") era. Equally important is Hitchcock's tendency to shoot alternative takes of scenes. This differed from coverage in that the films were not necessarily shot from varying angles so as to give the editor options to shape the film how they chose (often under the producer's aegis).[[282]](#cite_note-Heritage02-28-07-293)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Rather they represented Hitchcock's tendency to give himself options in the editing room, where he would provide advice to his editors after viewing a rough cut of the work.

According to Krohn, this and a great deal of other information revealed through his research of Hitchcock's personal papers, script revisions and the like refute the notion of Hitchcock as a director who was always in control of his films, whose vision of his films did not change during production, which Krohn notes has remained the central long-standing myth of Alfred Hitchcock. Both his fastidiousness and attention to detail also found their way into each [film poster](/wiki/Film_poster "Film poster") for his films. Hitchcock preferred to work with the best talent of his day—film poster designers such as [Bill Gold](/wiki/Bill_Gold "Bill Gold")[[283]](#cite_note-294) and [Saul Bass](/wiki/Saul_Bass "Saul Bass")—who would produce posters that accurately represented his films.[[281]](#cite_note-Raymond_Bellour_2000_p._217-292)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Legacy")]

### Awards and honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Awards and honours")]

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Alfred_Hitchcock "List of awards and nominations received by Alfred Hitchcock")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Hitchcock_walk_of_fame.jpg/250px-Hitchcock_walk_of_fame.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitchcock_walk_of_fame.jpg)

One of Hitchcock's stars on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame")

Hitchcock was inducted into the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") on 8 February 1960 with two stars: one for television and a second for motion pictures.[[284]](#cite_note-295) In 1978, [John Russell Taylor](/wiki/John_Russell_Taylor "John Russell Taylor") described him as "the most universally recognizable person in the world" and "a straightforward middle-class Englishman who just happened to be an artistic genius".[[239]](#cite_note-Ebert2Jan1980-248) In 2002, *[MovieMaker](/wiki/MovieMaker "MovieMaker")* named him the most influential director of all time,[[285]](#cite_note-MovieMaker-296) and a 2007 *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* critics' poll ranked him Britain's greatest director.[[286]](#cite_note-297) David Gritten, the newspaper's film critic, wrote: "Unquestionably the greatest filmmaker to emerge from these islands, Hitchcock did more than any director to shape modern cinema, which would be utterly different without him. His flair was for narrative, cruelly withholding crucial information (from his characters and from us) and engaging the emotions of the audience like no one else."[[287]](#cite_note-Avedon14April2007-298) In 1992, the *[Sight & Sound](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound "Sight & Sound")* Critics' Poll ranked Hitchcock at No. 4 in its list of "Top 10 Directors" of all time.[[288]](#cite_note-299) In 2002, Hitchcock was ranked second in the critics' top ten poll[[289]](#cite_note-300) and fifth in the directors' top ten poll[[290]](#cite_note-301) in the list of "The Greatest Directors of All Time" compiled by *Sight & Sound*. Hitchcock was voted the "Greatest Director of 20th Century" in a poll conducted by Japanese film magazine *[kinema Junpo](/wiki/Kinema_Junpo "Kinema Junpo")*. In 1996, *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* ranked Hitchcock at No. 1 in its "50 Greatest Directors" list.[[291]](#cite_note-auto-302)[[292]](#cite_note-303) Hitchcock was ranked at No. 2 on *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")*'s "Top 40 Greatest Directors of All-Time" list in 2005.[[291]](#cite_note-auto-302) In 2007, *[Total Film](/wiki/Total_Film "Total Film")* ranked Hitchcock at No. 1 on its "100 Greatest Film Directors Ever" list.[[293]](#cite_note-304)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Sir_Alfred_Hitchcock_%284313226125%29.jpg/250px-Sir_Alfred_Hitchcock_%284313226125%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Alfred_Hitchcock_(4313226125).jpg)

An [English Heritage](/wiki/English_Heritage "English Heritage") [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") marks where Hitchcock lived at 153 Cromwell Road, Kensington, London.

He won two [Golden Globes](/wiki/Golden_Globes "Golden Globes"), eight [Laurel Awards](/wiki/Laurel_Awards "Laurel Awards"), and five [lifetime achievement awards](/wiki/Lifetime_achievement_awards "Lifetime achievement awards"), including the first [BAFTA Academy Fellowship Award](/wiki/BAFTA_Academy_Fellowship_Award "BAFTA Academy Fellowship Award") in 1971,[[294]](#cite_note-305) and, in 1979, an [AFI Life Achievement Award](/wiki/AFI_Life_Achievement_Award "AFI Life Achievement Award").[[10]](#cite_note-Todd30April1980-13) He was nominated five times for an [Academy Award for Best Director](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Director "Academy Award for Best Director"). *Rebecca*, nominated for eleven Oscars, won the [Academy Award for Best Picture of 1940](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture#1940s "Academy Award for Best Picture"); another Hitchcock film, *[Foreign Correspondent](/wiki/Foreign_Correspondent_(film) "Foreign Correspondent (film)")*, was also nominated that year.[[295]](#cite_note-306) By 2021, nine of his films had been selected for preservation by the US [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry"): *Rebecca* (1940; inducted 2018), *Shadow of a Doubt* (1943; inducted 1991), *Notorious* (1946; inducted 2006), *Strangers on a Train* (1951; inducted 2021), *Rear Window* (1954; inducted 1997), *Vertigo* (1958; inducted 1989), *North by Northwest* (1959; inducted 1995), *Psycho* (1960; inducted 1992) and *The Birds* (1963; inducted 2016).[[8]](#cite_note-loc-9) In June 1968, Hitchcock was awarded an honorary Doctor of Fine Arts at the [Quarry Amphitheater](/wiki/Quarry_Amphitheater "Quarry Amphitheater") by the [University of California, Santa Cruz](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Cruz "University of California, Santa Cruz").[[296]](#cite_note-307)

In 2001, a series of 17 mosaics of Hitchcock's life and work, which are located in [Leytonstone tube station](/wiki/Leytonstone_tube_station "Leytonstone tube station") in the [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground"), was commissioned by the [London Borough of Waltham Forest](/wiki/London_Borough_of_Waltham_Forest "London Borough of Waltham Forest").[[297]](#cite_note-308) In 2012, Hitchcock was selected by artist [Sir Peter Blake](/wiki/Peter_Blake_(artist) "Peter Blake (artist)"), author of the Beatles' *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")* album cover, to appear in a new version of the cover, along with other British cultural figures, and he was featured that year in a [BBC Radio 4](/wiki/BBC_Radio_4 "BBC Radio 4") series, *[The New Elizabethans](/wiki/The_New_Elizabethans "The New Elizabethans")*, as someone "whose actions during the reign of Elizabeth II have had a significant impact on lives in these islands and given the age its character".[[298]](#cite_note-309) In June 2013 nine restored versions of Hitchcock's early silent films, including *The Pleasure Garden* (1925), were shown at the [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music")'s Harvey Theatre; known as "The Hitchcock 9", the travelling tribute was organised by the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute").[[299]](#cite_note-Kehr-310)

### Archives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Archives")]

The Alfred Hitchcock Collection is housed at the [Academy Film Archive](/wiki/Academy_Film_Archive "Academy Film Archive") in Hollywood, California. It includes home movies, 16mm film shot on the set of *Blackmail* (1929) and *Frenzy* (1972), and the earliest known colour footage of Hitchcock. The Academy Film Archive has preserved many of his home movies.[[300]](#cite_note-311) In 1984, [Pat Hitchcock](/wiki/Pat_Hitchcock "Pat Hitchcock") donated her father's papers to the academy's [Margaret Herrick Library](/wiki/Margaret_Herrick_Library "Margaret Herrick Library").[[301]](#cite_note-312)[[302]](#cite_note-313) The David O. Selznick and the Ernest Lehman collections housed at the Harry Ransom Humanities Research Center in Austin, Texas, contain material related to Hitchcock's work on the production of *The Paradine Case*, *Rebecca*, *Spellbound*, *North by Northwest* and *Family Plot.*[[303]](#cite_note-314)

## Notes and sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Notes and sources")]

### Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alfred_Hitchcock&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** According to Gene Adair (2002), Hitchcock made 53 feature films.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdair20029-2) According to [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") in 1980, it was 54.[[3]](#cite_note-Ebert1May1980-3)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** The films selected for the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") are *[Rebecca](/wiki/Rebecca_(1940_film) "Rebecca (1940 film)")* (1940), *[Shadow of a Doubt](/wiki/Shadow_of_a_Doubt "Shadow of a Doubt")* (1943), *[Notorious](/wiki/Notorious_(1946_film) "Notorious (1946 film)")* (1946), *[Strangers on a Train](/wiki/Strangers_on_a_Train_(film) "Strangers on a Train (film)")* (1951), *[Rear Window](/wiki/Rear_Window "Rear Window")* (1954), *[Vertigo](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)")* (1958), *[North by Northwest](/wiki/North_by_Northwest "North by Northwest")* (1959), *[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)")* (1960) and *[The Birds](/wiki/The_Birds_(film) "The Birds (film)")* (1963).[[8]](#cite_note-loc-9)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-12)** Alfred Hitchcock ([North American Newspaper Alliance](/wiki/North_American_Newspaper_Alliance "North American Newspaper Alliance"), 16 July 1972): "My own favorite is *Shadow of a Doubt*. You never saw it? Ah. It was written by [Thornton Wilder](/wiki/Thornton_Wilder "Thornton Wilder"). It's a character study, a suspense thriller. The beauty of the film was it was shot in the actual town."[[9]](#cite_note-11)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-50)** In his first story, "Gas" (June 1919), published in the first issue, a young woman is being assaulted by a mob of men in Paris, only to find she has been hallucinating in the dentist's chair.[[40]](#cite_note-43) This was followed by "The Woman's Part" (September 1919), which describes a husband watching his wife, an actor, perform on stage.[[41]](#cite_note-44) "Sordid" (February 1920) concerns an attempt to buy a sword from an antiques dealer, with another twist ending.[[42]](#cite_note-45) "And There Was No Rainbow" (September 1920) finds Bob caught *[in flagrante](/wiki/In_flagrante "In flagrante")* with a friend's wife.[[43]](#cite_note-46) In "What's Who?" (December 1920), confusion reigns when a group of actors impersonate themselves.[[44]](#cite_note-47) "The History of Pea Eating" (December 1920) is a satire on the difficulty of eating peas.[[45]](#cite_note-48) His final piece, "Fedora" (March 1921) describes an unknown woman: "small, simple, unassuming, and noiseless, yet she commands profound attention on all sides".[[46]](#cite_note-49)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-111)** In 2017, a *[Time Out](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* magazine poll ranked *Sabotage* as the 44th best British film ever.[[96]](#cite_note-Time_Out-100)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-148)** Hitchcock told [Bryan Forbes](/wiki/Bryan_Forbes "Bryan Forbes") in 1967: "They had gone through the film in my absence and taken out every scene that indicated the possibility that Cary Grant was a murderer. So there was no film existing at all. That was ridiculous. Nevertheless, I had to compromise on the end. What I wanted to do was that the wife was aware that she was going to be murdered by her husband, so she wrote a letter to her mother saying that she was very much in love with him, she didn't want live anymore, she was going to be killed but society should be protected. She therefore brings up this fatal glass of milk, drinks it and before she does she says, "Will you mail this letter to mother?" Then she drinks the milk and dies. You then have just one final scene of a cheerful Cary Grant going to the mailbox and posting the letter. ... But this was never permitted because of the basic error in casting."[[142]](#cite_note-HitchcockForbes-147)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-193)** A 2012 [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") poll ranked *Vertigo* as the [greatest film ever made](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound#2012 "Sight & Sound").[[7]](#cite_note-Christie2012-8)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-209)** A documentary on *Psycho*'s shower scene, *78/52*, was released in 2017, directed by [Alexandre O. Philippe](/wiki/Alexandre_O._Philippe "Alexandre O. Philippe"); the title refers to the scene's 78 camera setups and 52 cuts.[[201]](#cite_note-208)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-222)** Thomas McDonald (*The New York Times*, 1 April 1962): "Starring in the film are Rod Taylor, Suzanne Pleshette, Jessica Tandy and 'Tippi' Hedren. Hitchcock signed Miss Hedren, a New York model, to a contract after having seen her in a television commercial. He insisted that she enclose her first name in single quotation marks, but would not explain why."[[212]](#cite_note-McDonald1April1962-220)[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoto2008245-221)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-278)** In 1967, Hitchcock told Truffaut: "I think the most interesting women, sexually, are the English women. I feel that the English women, the Swedes, the northern Germans, and Scandinavians are a great deal more exciting than the Latin, the Italian, and the French women. Sex should not be advertised. An English girl, looking like a schoolteacher, is apt to get into a cab with you and, to your surprise, she'll probably pull a man's pants open. ... [W]ithout the element of *surprise* the scenes become meaningless. There's no possibility to *discover* sex."[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETruffaut1983224-277)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-282)** Hitchcock told Fallaci in 1963: "When they [actors] aren't cows, they're children: that's something else I've often said. And everyone knows that there are good children, bad children, and stupid children. The majority of actors, though, are stupid children. They're always quarreling, and they give themselves a lot of airs. The less I see of them, the happier I am. I had much less trouble directing fifteen hundred crows than one single actor. I've always said that Walt Disney has the right idea. His actors are made of paper; when he doesn't like them, he can tear them up."[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFallaci1963-195)
